# Studio - Full Documentation

# Access the Metadata Editor

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

There are several ways of accessing the Metadata editor in Anypoint Studio.

## Metadata Tab

1.  Select a component in your canvas to display its Mule Properties view.
2.  Select the Metadata tab on the componentâs Mule Properties view.
3.  Click the Add Metadata button to start defining the metadata of your payload, attributes, and
    variables.
4.  Select the Mule event component to which you want to define metadata.
5.  Click the Edit icon ![Edit icon](_images/access-metadata-editor-task-93b19.png) to open the
    Metadata Editor.

For example, this is the metadata tab for an HTTP Listener source:

![The metadata tab highlighted displaying the metadata of a flow](_images/access-metadata-editor-task-320a3.png)

> [!NOTE] If you edit inbound endpoint metadata, you can define outbound metadata.

## Transform Message Component

1.  Select a Transform Component in your canvas to display its Mule Properties view.
2.  Click the Define Metadata link in either the input or output columns of the component, depending
    on the metadata you want to define.  
    If the previous component in your flow is already defining output metadata, the input section of
    the Transform Component already recognizes it. You can right click the payload field and select
    Set Metadata.

    ![The set metadata option highlighted in a transform message component](_images/access-metadata-editor-task-b0be6.png)

When you set metadata for a Transform Message component, you can select a checkbox to wrap elements
in a collection. Selecting the wrap-elements option sets the metadata as a list, where each element
on the list is an instance of the structure you defined. So for example, if you set your metadata as
an XML structure and click this checkbox, metadata shows as a list that contains N instances of that
XML structure.

## Package Explorer View

1.  Select **Window > Show View > Package Explorer** if the package explorer isnât already
    displayed.
2.  Right click a project in the Package Explorer view.
3.  Select **Mule > Manage Metadata Types**.

## See Also

- [Create a Metadata Class](create-metadata-class-task)
- [Define Input and Output Structure of a Transformation](../mule-runtime/latest/transform-input-output-structure-transformation-studio-task)
---

# Add Another Output to the Transform Message Component

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

A single Transform Message component can shape different output parts, such as the payload, a flow
variable, or a property. You must define each output part in a separate DataWeave code, written out
in a separate tab of the Transform pane. For example: if one tab defines the payload, and another an
outbound property, both are parts of the same output Mule message.

To add a new output:

1.  Click the **Add new target** button
    ![Add new target icon](_images/using-dataweave-in-studio-34a3e.png).
2.  Specify where in the output Mule message to place the result of this new DataWeave transform. In
    case youâre creating a new variable or property, you must also set a name for it.

    ![The new-target window with options to select a variable name and source code](_images/dw-new-variable.png)

> [!NOTE] Execution order of multiple outputs can vary. Ensure each transformation is independent of
> the order of execution and the other outputs.

## See Also

- [Transform Message Component](transform-message-component-concept-studio)
- [Change the Target Output of a Transformation](change-target-output-transformation-studio-task)
- [Mule Message Structure](../mule-runtime/latest/about-mule-message)
- [DataWeave language](../mule-runtime/latest/dataweave)
---

# Add Custom Modules to Your Project

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

You can add your custom Mule module to your Mule projects in Anypoint Studio.

These instructions assume that you developed your Mule module using the Mule SDK, and that you
followed the proper setup steps.  
See the [Mule SDK introductory instructions](../mule-sdk/latest/getting-started) for more details.

You can install custom modules using the Studio UI, or by manually adding them as a Maven dependency
in your projectâs `pom.xml` file.

## From Studio

To install a local custom module from Studio:

1.  In the menu bar, select the **Manage dependencies** icon
    ![Manage dependencies icon](_images/manage-dependencies.png).
2.  Under **Mule Project**, select **Modules**:

    ![The Modules option highlighted in your Mule project](_images/manage-modules.png)

3.  Select the green plus icon (![A green plus sign](_images/add-icon.png)) and select **From
    Maven**:

    ![The from Maven option is highlighted in your Mule project properties window](_images/add-modules-from-maven.png)

4.  In the properties window, configure your custom moduleâs Maven coordinates:

    ![A Mule project property window showing fields to input artifact definition and advanced settings](_images/add-modules-maven-ui.png)

    This information is available in your moduleâs `pom.xml` file.

## From Maven

As you develop Mule modules as Maven projects, you can install them from a local or online Maven
repository:

1.  In your Anypoint Studio project, edit the `pom.xml` file with the groupId, artifactId, and
    version of your Mule module.  
    This information is available in your moduleâs `pom.xml` file. For example:

    ```xml
    <parent>
        <groupId>org.mule.extensions</groupId>
        <artifactId>mule-ee-core-modules-parent</artifactId>
        <version>1.1.0-SNAPSHOT</version>
    </parent>

    <groupId>com.mulesoft.mule.modules</groupId>
    <artifactId>mule-secure-configuration-property-module</artifactId>
    <packaging>mule-extension</packaging>
    <version>1.0.0-SNAPSHOT</version>

    <name>Mule Secure Configuration Property Extension</name>
    <description>Mule extension for loading encrypted properties</description>
    ```

    Itâs also reflected in the folder structure in which the Mule module is installed
    (`$MAVEN_REPOS_HOME/groupID_path/artifactId/version`).

    For the previous example, your Anypoint Studio pom.xml file must add the following dependency:

    ```xml
    <dependency>
    	<groupId>com.mulesoft.mule.modules</groupId>
    	<artifactId>mule-secure-configuration-property-module</artifactId>
    	<version>1.0.0-SNAPSHOT</version>
    	<classifier>mule-plugin</classifier>
    </dependency>
    ```

    The `<classifier>` element is set to mule-plugin. This automatically installs the mule-plugin
    into your projectâs palette.

2.  Save your changes to your Studio projectâs `pom.xml` file.

After Anypoint Studio downloads the new dependency, youâll see your new Mule module in the Studio
palette. In this example, there is a Secure Properties module listed in the Mule Palette view.  
Thereâs also a new Secure Properties Config element available in the Global Elements editor, and the
new XML namespace and XML tags are available in the XML configuration file editor.

## See Also

- [To Add Modules to Your Project](add-modules-in-studio-to)
- [Get Started with the Mule SDK](../mule-sdk/latest/getting-started)
---

# Add Modules to Your Project

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

You can add Modules and Connectors to your Mule project using the **Mule Palette**.

If youâre editing a Mule Application project:

1.  Go to the **Mule Palette**, and click **Search in Exchange**.

    By default, this list shows the available public modules. If you configured your Anypoint
    Platform credentials in Studio, you can also see your organizationâs private modules.  
    If you have no credentials configured in Studio, you can click **Add Account** to access your
    organizationâs modules.

2.  Select your desired modules from the **Available Modules** list by clicking **Add >**.
3.  Click **Finish**.

The selected module is now part of the **Mule Palette**.

Additionally, you can also select the **Add Modules** option, and drag your desired module from the
featured list.

Modules are dependencies of your Mule project. Adding a module to a Mule project in Studio doesnât
make that module available to other projects in your Studio workspace.

If youâre editing a Mule domain project:

1.  Go to **Global Elements** from your Mule domain configuration editor and click **Manage
    Modules**.
2.  Click **Add Module**.

    By default, this list shows the available public modules. If you configured your Anypoint
    Platform credentials in Studio, you can also see your organizationâs private modules.  
    If you have no credentials configured in Studio, you can click **Add Account** to access your
    organizationâs modules.

3.  Select your desired modules from the **Available Modules** list by clicking **Add >**.
4.  Click **Finish**.

The selected Module is now part of your Mule Domain and it shows up in the **Mule Palette** of your
Mule application.

> [!NOTE] Modules inherited by a Mule domain show as such in the Mule Palette view.  
> You canât edit or delete domain modules from your Mule application palette.

## See Also

- [To Add Custom Modules to Your Project](add-custom-modules-in-studio-to)
- [To Configure Anypoint Platform Credentials](set-credentials-in-studio-to)
---

# API Development in Studio

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Ensure that your Mule applications always refer to a single source of truth for your API
specifications. Design, publish, and implement your API specifications with the following workflow:

1.  Design an API specification.

    You can either create and publish an API specification with the
    [Visual Editor](../design-center/design-create-publish-api-visual-editor) or with the
    [Text Editor](../design-center/design-create-publish-api-raml-editor).

    You can also
    [create an API specification project in Studio and sync it with MuleSoft VCS](create-api-specification-studio).

2.  Publish your API specification to Exchange.

    Make your newly designed API available to your entire organization as an Exchange dependency. As
    you change your API, you can publish new versions of your API specifications to Exchange. See
    [best practices for API versioning](../exchange/to-change-raml-version#best-practices-api-versioning).

3.  Import and implement the API specification from Exchange to Studio.  
    You can either import and implement an API specification from
    [Exchange](import-api-specification-exchange) or from [Maven](import-api-specification-maven).
    Studio imports your API specification as a dependency. This means that your Mule application
    project now references the specific API version that youâre implementing. Studio checks your API
    and notifies you when thereâs a new version available.

This workflow applies to AsyncAPI, OAS, and RAML API specifications. AsyncAPI specifications require
Studio 7.18.0 or later, and Mule runtime engine 4.5.0 or later. OAS and RAML API specifications
require Studio 7.8.x or later, and Mule runtime engine 4.1.4 or later.

With Studio 7.18 and later, you can
[import an AsyncAPI specification from Exchange](import-api-specification-exchange) and implement it
in your Mule project.

## API Specifications as Exchange Dependencies

Manage APIs for your Mule application the same way you manage the modules that compose your
application. By leveraging Exchange as your unified repository for all your Mule application
dependencies, you can check the latest versions of your dependencies, ensuring that your application
is up-to-date with the latest API requirements.

Because the implementation of your API can change with each iteration, you must keep track so that
you can update your Mule application to match newer specifications. To help you track these changes,
treat your API specifications as dependencies of your project. This way, every time the
specification design changes, the API publishes a new API version that you can manage from Studio.
You can also update your application to match the new API version.

Each API version that you import from Exchange is stored as a reference to the API version
dependency in Exchange, rather than as a local copy in your projectâs structure. By referencing the
API version in Exchange, Studio can periodically check the status of the version and notify you when
a new version is available.

### Scaffold AsyncAPI, RAML, and OAS API Specifications

Design your API using AsyncAPI, OAS, or RAML specifications in Anypoint Design Center:

- [Create an API Specification with the Visual API Editor](../design-center/design-create-publish-api-visual-editor)
- [Create an API Specification with the Text Editor](../design-center/design-create-publish-api-raml-editor)

> [!NOTE] You can design an AsyncAPI API in Design Center with the
> [Text Editor](../design-center/design-create-publish-api-raml-editor).

When you import these specifications as dependencies of your flows, you can automatically generate
(scaffold) the flows in your canvas based on the endpoints described in the API specification.

Studio leverages APIkit to scaffold the flow and make it easier for you to start developing a Mule
application from an API specification.

> [!NOTE] Studio doesnât currently support scaffolding API specs that reference OAS, JSON schema, or
> AsyncAPI fragments imported from Exchange as project dependencies. To scaffold a spec with these
> fragments, you must provide the fragments inline within the spec or through fragment files that
> you create manually within your project directory. This limitation doesnât affect RAML fragments
> from Exchange, which are fully supported.

## API Specifications as Local Copies in Your Projects

Import a RAML API specification either [from your hard drive](import-api-specification-local-file)
or [directly from MuleSoft VCS](import-api-specification-design-center) into the `/src/main/api/`
directory in the structure of your project. This process creates a local copy of the API in your
projectâs structure. The imported API works as a standalone attachment to your project, meaning that
every additional upgrade requires a manual import.

When the API imported as a local copy into your projectâs structure is updated outside your Studio
environment, reimport the API into your project. Studio regenerates all the flows based on the
updated specification.

> [!NOTE] Duplicated copies of your specifications can be created on your local drive. You can only
> keep RAML API specifications as local copies within your projectâs structure.

## API Specifications as API Specification Projects in Studio

To start an API specification project in Studio and sync it with MuleSoft VCS, see
[Create an API Specification Project in Studio](create-api-specification-studio). Studio uses
version control system (VCS) capabilities to keep your API specifications synchronized between your
Studio workspace and MuleSoft VCS.

Import an API specification from MuleSoft VCS as an API specification project with VCS-enabled
capabilities to edit your API specification using Studio and sync your changes with MuleSoft VCS:

1.  Design an API specification in Design Center.

    You can either create and publish an API specification with the
    [Visual Editor](../design-center/design-create-publish-api-visual-editor) or with the
    [Text Editor](../design-center/design-create-publish-api-raml-editor).

2.  Import the API specification from Anypoint Platform through MuleSoft VCS into Studio.

    You can import the API specification as an editable asset by
    [Synchronizing an API Specifications with MuleSoft VCS](sync-api-projects-design-center).

You can also edit an API specification that you imported as an API dependency in your project:

1.  Import the API specification [from Exchange to Studio](import-api-specification-exchange).

    Studio imports your API specification as a dependency. This means that your Mule application
    project now references the specific API version that youâre implementing. Studio monitors your
    API and notifies you when thereâs a new version available.

2.  Select the API specification imported in your project and
    [Edit your API specification from Studio](sync-imported-api-specifications-design-center) to
    later sync your changes to MuleSoft VCS.

> [!NOTE] Studio uses the EGit plugin to edit API specifications offline. Studio doesnât support the
> EGit plugin for Mule application projects tracked with your own VCS.

## See Also

- [Create an API Specification Project in Studio](create-api-specification-studio)
- [Import and Implement an API Spec](import-api-specification)
- [Tracking the Number of Billable Flows in Studio](flow-counting-studio)
- [Test Your API Specification Using the API Console](test-specification-api-console)
---

# Search Inside Your API Specification Dependencies

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

When you import your API specifications from Exchange or Maven as dependencies of your projects,
those dependencies relocate from `src/resources/api/` to a ZIP file in your projectâs classpath.
This change means that you canât search for your API dependencies by using the File Search option in
Studio. To enable you to search for specific text inside your API dependencies, Studio introduces
the API Search feature.

You can access the API Search menu from the taskbar at the Anypoint Studio display, either by
selecting **Search** > **Searchâ¦â** or by pressing `Ctrl+h`:

![The API search tab highlighted in the search window](_images/api-search.png)

To search inside of your API specification dependencies:

1.  Type the text that you want to search for, following these conventions:
    - Use `*` to search for any chain of characters in your search criteria.  
      For example, `/*customer` matches both possible endpoints `/customers` and `/{customerId}`.
    - Append `?` to search for any character after a specific pattern.  
      For example, `/?customer` matches `/{customerId}` but not `/customers` because there must be a
      character between the search criteria that you defined between the `?` literal.
    - Use `\` to escape any of the reserved literals used in search patterns.  
      For example, if you want to search for strings containing `*`, `?`, or `\` you must escape
      them: `\*`, `\?`, and `\\`.

2.  If you want to specify a specific name pattern for the files you want to search through, type
    the pattern in the **File name patterns** field, following these conventions:
    - Use `*` to search for any chain of characters in your search criteria.  
      For example, `*.raml` searches in all file names that end with the `.raml` extension.
    - Use `?` to allow any character after a specific pattern.  
      For example, `customer?1.raml` searches inside file such as `customer01.raml`,
      `customer11.raml`, or `customer21.raml`.
    - Use `!` to exclude certain filenames or patterns.  
      For example, `!examples.raml` searches inside all files except `examples.raml`.

3.  Click the **Chooseâ¦â** button to filter per file type.
4.  Select one of the following types of API specifications in your project:
    - **API dependencies** searches inside API specifications imported from Exchange or Maven.
    - **Local API Files** searches inside API specifications added from a local file or from
      MuleSoft VCS.

5.  Click **Search**.

You can also search for all API specifications inside your open projects. You can select
**Navigate** > **Open API Resource** in the taskbar at the Anypoint Studio display, or press
`Cmd+Shift+p` (MacOS) or `Ctrl+Shift+P` (Windows/Linux):

![API search field highlighted in the Open API Resource window](_images/open-api-resource.png)

1.  Type the name of the API resource that youâre looking for:

    ![API resource search field highlighted in the Open API Resource window](_images/open-api-resource-result.png)

    Studio displays all possible matches for the API resource that you typed.

2.  Select the resource you were looking for and click **OK**.
---

# Sync Your API Projects with API Sync

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

With API Sync, you can:

- Start designing a RAML, OAS 2.0, or OAS 3.0 API spec in Studio and sync it with API Sync.
- Pull a published RAML, OAS 2.0, or OAS 3.0 API spec from Exchange or from a private Maven
  repository, into your Mule application project in Studio to scaffold a new project.
- Edit the API spec you imported from Exchange, and push your changes to MuleSoft VCS.
- Scaffold multiple flows in different Mule configuration files by using API Sync on more than one
  API spec.
- Monitor the API versions that you implement in your project and update your flows based on the
  latest published version.
- Pull a RAML, OAS 2.0, or OAS 3.0 API spec from MuleSoft VCS into Studio, edit the spec offline,
  and push the updates back to MuleSoft VCS.
- Develop your Mule applications using API lifecycle development practices.

API Sync supports importing both RAML, OAS 2.0, and OAS 3.0 spec.

API Sync is bundled by default with Studio and requires Mule 4.1.4 or later.

API Sync doesnât work properly if the RAML file exceeds 4,000 lines of text.

## Create an API Spec in Studio

Start a new API spec project in Studio. See
[Create an API Specification Project in Studio](create-api-specification-studio) for more
information.

## Import an API Spec from Exchange

Import an API spec from Exchange into a new Mule project or from MuleSoft VCS into an editable API
spec project.

See [Import and Implement an API Specification from Exchange](import-api-specification-exchange) for
more information.

See [Update the API Spec in Your Project](sync-update-api-spec) for more information about keeping
your Studio projects up to date with the latest version of an API spec.

See [Synchronize API Specifications with MuleSoft VCS](sync-api-projects-design-center) for more
information.

### Edit the Imported API Spec in Studio

Edit the imported API spec in your project as an API spec project.

1.  Import the API spec from Exchange to your Studio project.
2.  Sync it to MuleSoft VCS.
3.  Publish it to Exchange.
4.  Import the new version of your API spec.

See
[Edit Imported API Specifications in Your Projects in Studio](sync-imported-api-specifications-design-center)
for more information.

## See Also

- [API Development in Studio](api-development-studio)
- [Import and Implement an API Specification from Exchange](import-api-specification-exchange)
- [Import and Implement an API Specification from Maven](import-api-specification-maven)
---

# Breakpoints View Reference

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

The **Mule Breakpoints** view displays all breakpoints defined in all the Mule projects opened in
Studio.

![Breakpoint elements highlighted in the Mule Breakpoints View](_images/breakpoints-view.png)

<table><tbody><tr><td><i data-value="1"></i><b>1</b></td><td><strong>Mute breakpoints</strong><br>Temporarily disables all breakpoints</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="2"></i><b>2</b></td><td><strong>Disable error breakpoint</strong><br>Enables error breakpoints<br>Any event processor in the application becomes a breakpoint if it throws an error.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="3"></i><b>3</b></td><td><strong>Remove all breakpoints</strong><br>When selected, removes all breakpoints but doesnât remove the event processors under the breakpoint</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="4"></i><b>4</b></td><td><strong>Remove breakpoint</strong><br>Removes the selected breakpoint</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="5"></i><b>5</b></td><td><strong>Conditional</strong><br>Sets the condition to evaluate for the selected breakpoint<br>The condition must be a <code>boolean</code>.</td></tr></tbody></table>

## See Also

- [Breakpoints](breakpoints-concepts)
---

# Breakpoints

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

You can add breakpoints to your message processors so that the Studio debugger stops the execution
of your flow at that particular processor. You can later inspect the content of the message as it
exists at that point in the flow.

A breakpoint stops the execution of the flow when the message reaches it. You can also configure a
breakpoint to execute when a specific condition in the message is met, or whenever an error occurs.
See the following types of breakpoints for more details.

## Default Breakpoints

When you add a breakpoint, Studio enables the breakpoint by default.

To add a default breakpoint, right-click a message processor and select **Add Breakpoint**:

![The Add breakpoint option highlighted in the context menu of a flow](_images/add-breakpoint.png)

To disable the breakpoint, right-click the message processor and select **Disable Breakpoint**:

![The Disable breakpoint option highlighted in the context menu of a flow](_images/disable-breakpoint.png)

Although you disable the breakpoint, Studio does not delete it. In the canvas, the breakpointâs
corresponding event processor displays a gray circle instead of a red circle:

![A gray circle shows a disabled breakpoint](_images/disabled-breakpoint.png)

When you have many breakpoints, you can use the **Mule Breakpoints** view in the **Mule Debug**
perspective. The **Mule Breakpoints** view lists all the message processors that have breakpoints
and indicates with a check mark all the enabled breakpoints. You can select and unselect the check
marks to enable or disable the breakpoints you need:

![The elements of the Mule Breakpoints view in the Mule Debug perspective](_images/breakpoint-activation.png)

When you run your application in debug mode, Studio stops the flow execution at the breakpoint you
added, enabling you to inspect the messageâs content in the **Mule Debugger** view.

## Conditional Breakpoints

A conditional breakpoint activates when a predefined condition evaluates to `true` when Studio
executes the event processor with the breakpoint. You define the condition using a DataWeave
expression.

To add a conditional breakpoint:

1.  Right-click the message processor and select **Add Breakpoint**:

    ![The Add breakpoint option highlighted in the context menu of a flow](_images/add-breakpoint.png)

2.  In the **Mule Breakpoints** view, select the breakpoint to which you want to add a condition,
    and select **Conditional**:

    ![Set Payload and Conditional elements highlighted In the Mule Breakpoints view](_images/set-conditional-breakpoint.png)

3.  Enter the condition that must be met to activate the breakpoint.  
    For example, the following expression activates the breakpoint if the method used in the request
    is `GET`:

    ```none
    attributes.method == "GET"
    ```

You can change the condition without stopping the applicationâs execution.

## Error Breakpoints

Enabling error breakpoints causes any event processor in the application to become a breakpoint if
it throws an error. Studio stops the flow execution whenever an error occurs. The **Mule Debugger**
view displays the content of the message as it exists at that point in the flow that the error
occurred.

Studio enables error breakpoints by default.

You canât add specific error breakpoints to stop the flow execution when a specific error occurs.

To disable error breakpoints, select the **Disable error breakpoints**
(![Disable error breakpoints icon](_images/to-set-error-breakpoints-55f71.png)") button in the
**Mule Breakpoints** view:

![Disable error breakpoints button highlighted in the Mule Breakpoints view](_images/disable-error-breakpoint.png)

## Temporarily Mute Breakpoints

You can activate the **Mute Breakpoints** option in the **Mule Breakpoints** view to temporarily
disable the debugger from stopping at your enabled breakpoints:

![The Mute breakpoints icon highlighted in the Mule Breakpoints view](_images/mute-breakpoints.png)

By default, muting your breakpoints also disables the error breakpoints. You can configure this
behavior in **Preferences** > **Anypoint Studio** > **Debugger** by unselecting the **Mute
Breakpoints disables Error Breakpoints** check mark:

![The Mute breakpoints disables Error Breakpoints checkbox highlighted in the Anypoint Studio Preferences menu](_images/mute-disable-error-breakpoints.png)
---

# Configure Studio Browser Settings

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Anypoint Studio comes bundled with a default browser to display elements of the Anypoint Platform
UI. For example, when you deploy to CloudHub from Studio, Studio displays the Runtime Manager UI by
using a built-in browser.

This built-in browser is the default. However, if you log in to Anypoint Platform using multi-factor
authentication (MFA), you must configure Studio to use your systemâs native browser to render the
Anypoint Platform UI:

1.  Navigate to **Preferences** > **Anypoint Studio** > **Browser**.
2.  Select the type of default browser for Studio:

    ![The browser preferences window highlighting built-in or native options](_images/browser-configuration.png)

<table><tbody><tr><td><i data-value="1"></i><b>1</b></td><td><strong>Built-in</strong>: Uses the built-in browser.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="2"></i><b>2</b></td><td><strong>Native</strong>: Uses the native browser of your operative system.</td></tr></tbody></table>

3.  Click **Preview** to verify the changed configuration.

Additionally, if you use Windows you must perform an additional configuration:

1.  Quit Anypoint Studio if youâre running it.
2.  Download the latest fixed version of WebView2 from the
    [Microsoft Developer Portal](https://developer.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoft-edge/webview2/?form=MA13LH).
3.  Extract the `.cab` file with the following command in a Command Prompt (CMD):
    `Extrac32 /E <path_to_CAB>`
4.  Locate your `AnypointStudio.ini` file inside your Anypoint Studio installation directory.
5.  Add the following lines to the `AnypointStudio.ini` file:

    ```none
    ---
    -Dorg.eclipse.swt.browser.DefaultType=edge
    -Dorg.eclipse.swt.browser.EdgeDir=<path-to-extracted-WebView2-dir>
    # for example:
    # -Dorg.eclipse.swt.browser.EdgeDir=C:\Users\my-user\Downloads\Microsoft.WebView2.FixedVersionRuntime.9x.x.xx.xx
    ---
    ```

## See Also

- [Configure Platform Credentials](set-credentials-in-studio-to)
- [Configure Proxy Settings](proxy-settings-task)
---

# Selecting a Different Java Version to Run the Embedded Mule Runtime

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

You can select any of your installed JREs as the default Java runtime environment that Studio uses
to run your Mule project.

You can select the version of the JDK to use for the project that is compatible with the Mule
runtime version the project runs on.

If you change the Java or Mule runtime versions in your project, consider these facts:

- Mule runtime 4.4 and earlier works with Java 8 and 11.
- Mule runtime 4.6 works with Java 8, 11, and 17.
- Standard support for Java 8 and 11 ends in March 2025 for Mule 4.8 Edge and August 2025 for 4.6
  LTS, so plan your upgrade path for apps that are running on Java 8 or 11 accordingly.
- Studio 7.21 and later builds and runs projects on Java 17 by default. To use Mule 4.9 and later,
  upgrade your apps to run on Java 17.

## Mule Runtime and Java Compatibility in Studio

This table shows the compatibility between Mule runtime and Java versions in Studio:

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Studio Version</th><th>Mule Runtime Version</th><th>Java 8</th><th>Java 11</th><th>Java 17</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>7.22 and later</p></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p>4.10.x</p></li><li><p>4.9.x (LTS) and later</p></li><li><p>4.8.x</p></li><li><p>4.6.x (LTS)</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>No</p></td><td><p>No</p></td><td><p>Yes</p></td></tr><tr><td rowspan="4"><p>7.20</p></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p>4.8.x</p></li><li><p>4.6.x (LTS)</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>Yes</p></td><td><p>Yes</p></td><td><p>Yes</p></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><ul><li><p>4.4.x</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>Yes</p></td><td><p>Yes</p></td><td><p>No</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

See the [Studio release notes](studio-release-notes) for the latest information about compatibility.

## Select the Java Version for All Your Studio Projects

1.  In Studio, click **Anypoint Studio**, and **Preferences**.
2.  Under **Java**, **Installed JREs**, select the JRE version to use.

    ![A table listing installed Java runtime environments with names, locations, and types](_images/installed-jres.png)

    If your installed JRE version doesnât appear, you must configure it:
    1.  Go to **Add** and select your installed JRE type:
        - `MacOS X VM` for macOS.
        - `Standard VM` for macOS, Windows, and Linux.

    2.  Click **Next**.
    3.  In **JRE Home**, click **Directory** and select the folder where you have installed JRE.  
        For example, `/Library/Java/JavaVirtualMachines/jdk-11.0.3.jdk/Contents/Home`
    4.  In **JRE Name** type in a descriptive name for the JRE.  
        For example, `Java SE 11`.
    5.  Click **Finish**.

## Migrate Your Project to a Different Java Version

Studio 7.17.0 and later are compatible with Java 17. Upgrade modules and apps in your projects to
obtain its latest features.

1.  Right-click your Studio project.
2.  Go to **Build Path**, and click **Configure Build Path**.
3.  In the **Libraries** tab, select your **JRE System Library** and click **Edit**.
4.  Select the **Alternate JRE** option, and select your desired JRE from the dropdown menu.

    ![Edit Library window displaying the alternate JRE options.](_images/change-jdk-project.png)

If you select Java 17 for your project, Studio automatically searches Exchange and suggests
[the modules and connectors to upgrade](update-modules) to make your app compatible with Java 17.

If you find issues, you can [troubleshoot them](compatibility-issues-runtime-java) and then
[deploy your project to the appropriate environment](deploy-mule-application-task) with Java
compatibility checks.

## Verify Java 17 Tooling

In Studio 7.19.0 and later, your new Mule projects use JDK 17 by default and the bundled modules in
your Mule projects must be Java 17 compatible. If you have issues with the modules that use previous
versions of Java:

1.  Go to **Settings** > **Anypoint Studio** > **Tooling**.
2.  In the **Life cycle** section, select Java 17 as the Java virtual machine for Studio services.
3.  Click **Apply and Close**.

    The Studio services restart and the tooling instance restarts using Java 17.

Use the [Java 17 module compatibility tool](#verify-java-17-module-compatibility) to verify the
modules in your Mule project are Java 17 compatible.

## Verify Java 17 Module Compatibility

In Studio 7.19.0 and later, you can check if the modules in your Mule project are Java 17
compatible:

1.  Right-click the name of your project.
2.  Select **Mule** > **Java 17 Module Compatibility**.

    Studio analyzes the modules in your Mule project and shows the modules that arenât Java 17
    compatible in the **Problems** tab.

3.  [Update the modules](update-modules) that arenât Java 17 compatible.

![The problems tab is open, with one warning and three Java module compatibility notifications](_images/java-17-tooling.png)

## Configure FIPS 140-2 in Studio

Studio supports FIPS 140-2 security model for Java 8, 11, and 17.

### Configure FIPS 140-2 in Java 8

1.  Download the provider files from the
    [BouncyCastle](https://www.bouncycastle.org/download/bouncy-castle-java-fips/) website.
    - Provider: bc-fips-1.0.2.4
    - TLS: bctls-fips-1.0.19.jar
    - PKIX: bcpkix-fips-1.0.7.jar

2.  Install the required JAR files into your Studio embedded JDK 8. This example shows the command
    for a default macOS installation. The path to the `â¦â/jre/lib/ext/` directory can be different
    depending on your operating system and Studio version.

    ```bash
    cp bc-fips-1.0.2.4.jar bctls-fips-1.0.19.jar bcpkix-fips-1.0.7.jar /Applications/AnypointStudio.app/Contents/Eclipse/plugins/org.mule.tooling.jdk.v8.macosx.x86_64_1.3.4/Contents/Home/jre/lib/ext/
    ```

    The default path on Windows is
    `C:\AnypointStudio\plugins\org.mule.tooling.jdk.v8.win32.x86_1.3.4\jre\lib\ext`.

3.  Register the security providers in the `java.security` properties file, located in your JDK 8
    `â¦â/lib/security/` folder. Bouncy Castle must be the only provider.

    ```java
    #
    # List of providers and their preference orders:
    #
    security.provider.1=org.bouncycastle.jcajce.provider.BouncyCastleFipsProvider
    security.provider.2=org.bouncycastle.jsse.provider.BouncyCastleJsseProvider fips:BCFIPS
    # ...
    ```

4.  Save your changes.
5.  In Studio, go to your project, right click its folder, and select **Run configuration**.
6.  Go to the **Arguments** tab. In **VM arguments**, add:

    ```text
    -Dmule.security.model=fips140-2
    -Dorg.bouncycastle.fips.approved_only=true
    ```

7.  Run your application. The startup readout shows FIPS security model enabled.

    ```text
    * Mule Runtime and Integration Platform
    * Version: 4.6.21 Build: adffffac
    * MuleSoft, Inc.
    * For more information go to
    * https://www.mulesoft.com/platform/soa/mule-esb-enterprise
    *
    * Server started: 6/4/18 4:06 PM
    * JDK: 1.8.0_172 (mixed mode)
    * JDK properties:
      - java.vendor = Oracle Corporation
      - java.vm.name = Java HotSpot(TM) 64-Bit Server VM
      - java.home = /Library/Java/JavaVirtualMachines/jdk1.8.0_172.jdk/Contents/Home/jre
    * OS: Mac OS X (10.11, x86_64)
    * Host: your-hostname.local (10.250.0.81)
    * Security model: fips140-2 (1)
    * Mule services:
      - api-gateway-contract-service-1.0.0-mule-service.jar
      - mule-service-http-ee-1.1.1-mule-service.jar
      - mule-service-oauth-1.1.1-mule-service.jar
      - mule-service-scheduler-1.1.4-mule-service.jar
      - mule-service-soap-1.1.2-mule-service.jar
      - mule-service-weave-ee-2.1.2-mule-service.jar
    * Mule system properties:
      - mule.security.model = fips140-2
      - mule.base = /Users/your-user/AnypointStudio/studio-workspace/.mule
      - mule.home = /Users/your-user/AnypointStudio/studio-workspace/.mule
    ```

    <table><tbody><tr><td><i data-value="1"></i><b>1</b></td><td>FIPS 140-2 security model is enabled.</td></tr></tbody></table>

### Configure FIPS 140-2 in Java 11 and Later

1.  Download the provider files from the
    [BouncyCastle](https://www.bouncycastle.org/download/bouncy-castle-java-fips/) website:
    - Provider: bc-fips-1.0.2.4
    - TLS: bctls-fips-1.0.19.jar
    - PKIX: bcpkix-fips-1.0.7.jar

2.  Copy the downloaded files to the embedded Mule runtime `â¦â/lib/boot` directory. The path to this
    directory can vary depending on your Studio and Mule runtime versions.

    ```bash
    cp bc-fips-1.0.2.4.jar bctls-fips-1.0.19.jar bcpkix-fips-1.0.7.jar /Applications/AnypointStudio.app/Contents/Eclipse/plugins/org.mule.tooling.server.4.9.ee_7.21.0.202508051502/mule/lib/boot
    ```

    The default path on Windows is
    `C:\AnypointStudio\plugins\org.mule.tooling.server.4.9.ee_7.21.0.202508051502\mule\lib\boot`.

3.  Delete the **wrapper.conf** file at
    `AnypointStudio\plugins\org.mule.tooling.server.4.9.ee_7.21.0.202508051502\mule\conf`.
4.  Edit
    `AnypointStudio\plugins\org.mule.tooling.server.4.9.ee_7.21.0.202508051502\mule\conf\wrapper.conf.template`
    and uncomment this line by deleting the starting **#** character:

    ```text
    wrapper.java.additional.21=--add-opens=java.base/sun.security.provider=org.bouncycastle.fips.core
    ```

5.  Edit
    `/Applications/AnypointStudio.app/Contents/Eclipse/plugins/org.mule.tooling.jdk.macosx.aarch64_1.4.1/Contents/Home/conf/security/java.security`
    to list only the BouncyCastle security providers.

    ```java
    security.provider.1=org.bouncycastle.jcajce.provider.BouncyCastleFipsProvider
    security.provider.2=org.bouncycastle.jsse.provider.BouncyCastleJsseProvider fips:BCFIPS
    ```

6.  In Studio, go to your project, right click its folder, and select **Run configuration**.
7.  Go to the **Arguments** tab. In **VM arguments**, add:

    ```text
    -Dmule.security.model=fips140-2
    -Dorg.bouncycastle.fips.approved_only=true
    ```

8.  Run your application. The startup readout shows FIPS security model enabled.

    ```text
    * Mule Runtime and Integration Platform
    * Version: 4.9.8 Build: adffffac
    * MuleSoft, Inc.
    * For more information go to
    * https://www.mulesoft.com/platform/soa/mule-esb-enterprise
    *
    * Server started: 8/10/21 5:12 PM
    * JDK: 11.0.11 (mixed mode)
    * JDK properties:
      - java.vendor = AdoptOpenJDK
      - java.vm.name = OpenJDK 64-Bit Server VM
      - java.home = /Library/Java/JavaVirtualMachines/adoptopenjdk-11.jdk/Contents/Home
    * OS: Mac OS X (11.5, x86_64)
    * Host: your-hostname.local (192.168.1.10)
    * Security model: fips140-2 (1)
    * Mule services:
      - ...
    * Mule system properties:
      - mule.security.model = fips140-2
      - mule.base = /Users/your-user/AnypointStudio/studio-workspace/.mule
      - mule.home = /Users/your-user/AnypointStudio/studio-workspace/.mule
    ```

    <table><tbody><tr><td><i data-value="1"></i><b>1</b></td><td>FIPS 140-2 security model is enabled.</td></tr></tbody></table>

## See Also

- [Java Support](../general/java-support)
- [Troubleshooting Issues with Mule Runtime and Java](compatibility-issues-runtime-java)
- [FIPS 140-2 Compliance Support](../mule-runtime/latest/fips-140-2-compliance-support)
---

# Configure Studio to Use Your Own JDK (Linux)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

1.  Locate your `AnypointStudio.ini` file in `/usr/lib/anypointstudio/anypointstudio.ini`.
2.  Add the `-vm` option to point to your specific JDK installation path.

    ```text
    -vm
    /opt/jdk-11.0.11+9/bin/java
    ```

    - The `-vm` option and the installation path must be on separate lines.
    - The value must be the full absolute or relative path to the Java executable, not just to the
      Java home directory.
    - The `-vm` option must occur after the other Studio-specific options (such as `-product`,
      `--launcher.*`, and so on), but before the `-vmargs` option.
---

# Configure Studio to Use Your Own JDK (MacOS)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

1.  Go to your `/Applications` folder, right click the `AnypointStudio` package and select **Show
    Package Contents**.
2.  Go to `Contents` > `Eclipse`, and open the `AnypointStudio.ini` file.
3.  Add the -vm option to point to your specific JDK installation path.

    ```text
    -vm
    /Library/Java/JavaVirtualMachines/jdk-11.0.11+9/Contents/Home/bin/java
    ```

    - The `-vm` option and the installation path must be on separate lines.
    - The value must be the full absolute or relative path to the Java executable, not just to the
      Java home directory.
    - The `-vm` option must occur after the other Studio-specific options (such as `-product`,
      `--launcher.*`, and so on), but before the `-vmargs` option.
---

# Configure Studio to Use Your Own JDK (Windows)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

1.  Locate your `AnypointStudio.ini` file inside your Anypoint Studio installation directory.
2.  Add the `-vm` option to point to your specific JDK installation path.

    ```text
    -vm
    C:\Program Files\Java\jdk-11.0.11+9\bin\javaw.exe
    ```

    - The `-vm` option and the installation path must be on separate lines.
    - The value must be the full absolute or relative path to the Java executable, not just to the
      Java home directory.
    - The `-vm` option must occur after the other Studio-specific options (such as `-product`,
      `--launcher.*`, and so on), but before the `-vmargs` option.

      If you encounter **Java was started but returned exit code=1** while starting Anypoint Studio,
      modify the `-vm` argument to point to the `jvm.dll`:

      ```text
      -vm
      C:\Program Files\Java\jdk-11.0.11+9\jre\bin\server\jvm.dll
      ```
---

# Configure Studio to Use Your Own JDK

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Anypoint Studio comes bundled with a Java development kit (JDK).

You can find the JDK version used by Studio under **About Anypoint Studio** > **Installation
Details**.

To start Studio using a different JDK, follow the instructions based on your operating system:

- [Windows](change-jdk-for-studio-wx)
- [Linux](change-jdk-for-studio-lx)
- [MacOS](change-jdk-for-studio-ox)

## Select the Appropriate Java and Mule Runtime Versions

If you change the Java or Mule runtime versions in your project, consider these facts:

- Mule runtime 4.4 and earlier works with Java 8 and 11.
- Mule runtime 4.6 works with Java 8, 11, and 17.
- Standard support for Java 8 and 11 ends in March 2025 for Mule 4.8 Edge and August 2025 for 4.6
  LTS, so plan your upgrade path for apps that are running on Java 8 or 11 accordingly.
- Studio 7.21 and later builds and runs projects on Java 17 by default. To use Mule 4.9 and later,
  upgrade your apps to run on Java 17.

## Mule Runtime and Java Compatibility in Studio

This table shows the compatibility between Mule runtime and Java versions in Studio:

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Studio Version</th><th>Mule Runtime Version</th><th>Java 8</th><th>Java 11</th><th>Java 17</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>7.22 and later</p></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p>4.10.x</p></li><li><p>4.9.x (LTS) and later</p></li><li><p>4.8.x</p></li><li><p>4.6.x (LTS)</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>No</p></td><td><p>No</p></td><td><p>Yes</p></td></tr><tr><td rowspan="4"><p>7.20</p></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p>4.8.x</p></li><li><p>4.6.x (LTS)</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>Yes</p></td><td><p>Yes</p></td><td><p>Yes</p></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><ul><li><p>4.4.x</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>Yes</p></td><td><p>Yes</p></td><td><p>No</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

See the [Studio release notes](studio-release-notes) for the latest information about compatibility.

## See Also

- [Java Support](../general/java-support)
---

# Change the Target Output of a Transformation

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

By default, the Transform Message component outputs to the message payload. You can change this
target, so that the result of your transformation populates another part of the output such as a
property or variable.

1.  Click the **Edit Current Target** button
    ![Edit icon](_images/using-dataweave-in-studio-47984.png).
2.  Specify where in the output Mule message to place the result of your DataWeave transform. In
    case youâre creating a new variable or property, you must also set a name for it.

    ![The variable name and source code options highlighted in the selection dialog window](_images/dw-new-variable.png)

## See Also

- [Transform Message Component](transform-message-component-concept-studio)
- [Add Another Output Target](add-another-output-transform-studio-task)
- [Mule Message Structure](../mule-runtime/latest/about-mule-message)
- [DataWeave language](../mule-runtime/latest/dataweave)
---

# Troubleshooting Issues with Mule Runtime and Java

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

The modules in your project can have compatibility issues with Java or Mule runtime.

If you change the Java or Mule runtime versions in your project, consider these facts:

- Mule runtime 4.4 and earlier works with Java 8 and 11.
- Mule runtime 4.6 works with Java 8, 11, and 17.
- Standard support for Java 8 and 11 ends in March 2025 for Mule 4.8 Edge and August 2025 for 4.6
  LTS, so plan your upgrade path for apps that are running on Java 8 or 11 accordingly.
- Studio 7.21 and later builds and runs projects on Java 17 by default. To use Mule 4.9 and later,
  upgrade your apps to run on Java 17.

## Mule Runtime and Java Compatibility in Studio

This table shows the compatibility between Mule runtime and Java versions in Studio:

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Studio Version</th><th>Mule Runtime Version</th><th>Java 8</th><th>Java 11</th><th>Java 17</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>7.22 and later</p></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p>4.10.x</p></li><li><p>4.9.x (LTS) and later</p></li><li><p>4.8.x</p></li><li><p>4.6.x (LTS)</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>No</p></td><td><p>No</p></td><td><p>Yes</p></td></tr><tr><td rowspan="4"><p>7.20</p></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p>4.8.x</p></li><li><p>4.6.x (LTS)</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>Yes</p></td><td><p>Yes</p></td><td><p>Yes</p></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><ul><li><p>4.4.x</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><p>Yes</p></td><td><p>Yes</p></td><td><p>No</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

See the [Studio release notes](studio-release-notes) for the latest information about compatibility.

When you create a project, Studio uses its default JRE. When you select a module, Studio displays
the latest version of the modules compatible with your JRE and Mule runtime.

If you select a different JRE for your project and donât update your module to a compatible version,
your project fails at deployment.

To avoid deployment errors, you must [select a JRE](change-jdk-config-in-projects) that is
compatible with the current Mule runtime used in Studio. If needed,
[update your modules](update-modules) to select a compatible version.

## See Also

- [Java Support](../general/java-support)
---

# Configure Studio to Use Your Own Maven

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Anypoint Studio comes bundled with Maven but you can use Studio with an external Maven installation
as well.

1.  Open Studio
2.  Go to Studio preferences:
    - In macOS, go to **Anypoint Studio > Settings**.
    - In Windows, go to **Anypoint Studio > Preferences**.

3.  In the Preferences panel, go to **Anypoint Studio > Maven**.
4.  Deselect **Use embedded Maven Installation**.
5.  Click **Browse** next to the **Maven installation home directory** field.
6.  Select your Maven root installation path and click **Open**.
7.  Click **Test Maven Configuration** to verify your Maven configuration in Studio is correct.

    ![Maven options highlighted in the Anypoint Studio preferences window](_images/maven-preferences.png)

8.  Click **Apply and Close**.

## See Also

- [Maven Preferences Reference](maven-preferences-reference)
---

# Optimizing API Testing with MUnit Coverage Reports in Studio

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Run a coverage report from Anypoint Studio to obtain detailed insights of the effectiveness of MUnit
tests on your Mule applications.

The following configurations apply when you execute your MUnit tests in Studio. They donât apply
when running tests from Maven in a CI/CD environment. To run coverage reports from Maven, see
[Maven Configuration for Coverage](../munit/latest/coverage-maven-concept).

## Enable Coverage Reports in Anypoint Studio

To see which flows, and which percentage of event processors in those flows the test covers, click
the **Coverage** button:

![MUnit Coverage tab highlighted in the MUnit section](_images/using-munit-coverage-in-anypoint-studio.png)

The **Overall coverage** value represents the percentage of Mule application event processors that
the MUnit test executes.

Click **Generate Report** to get the report that details the flows and event processors in the Mule
configuration files included in the MUnit test.

![Files included in the report highlighted](_images/munit-coverage-report-in-anypoint-studio.png)

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Column</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p><strong>Resource</strong></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Name of the Mule configuration file</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><strong>Containers</strong></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Number of flows in the Mule configuration file</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><strong>Weight</strong></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Proportion of event processors within the Mule configuration file compared to the total number of event processors across all configuration files</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><strong>Coverage</strong></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Percentage of event processors in the Mule configuration file that the MUnit test executes</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

Additionally, you can click on each **Resource** to get a more specific and granular report of the
Event Processors executed in that resource:

![MUnit Coverage Report with the Granular report highlighted](_images/munit-granular-coverage-report-in-anypoint-studio.png)

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Column</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p><strong>Name</strong></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Name of the container</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><strong>Type</strong></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Type of the container</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><strong>Covered Processors</strong></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Processors in the container executed during the MUnit test</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><strong>Processors</strong></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Total amount of processors in the container</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><strong>Coverage</strong></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Percentage of the event processors in the container executed in the MUnit test</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Disable Coverage Reports

Disable the coverage calculation from your project run configuration so you test your flow without a
coverage report.

Right-click **Package Explorer** and choose **Run As** > **Run Configurations**. Select your MUnit
run configuration file to access your suite configuration, and deselect the **Run coverage** option
in your coverage settings.

![MUnit run and Run coverage highlighted](_images/disable-munit-coverage-in-anypoint-studio.png)

## Exclude XML Files from MUnit Coverage Report

Exclude one or more XML files from an MUnit Coverage report when testing your application. For
example, exclude a third-party XML file that doesnât comply with your quality standards to improve
your final report score.

1.  Go to the flow to change.
2.  Click **Run** > **Run Configuration**.
3.  Click the name of your project.
4.  In the **Ignored files** section, click **Add**.

    ![Add Ignored File window highlighted in the Test tab](_images/add-ignored-files.png)

5.  Type the name of the XML file to exclude from the Coverage report and click **OK**.
6.  Click **Apply** and **Run**.

## See Also

- [Coverage](../munit/latest/munit-coverage-report)
---

# Create an API Specification Project in Studio

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Studio enables you to create a RAML API specification, an OAS 2.0 or 3.0 API specification, or an
API fragment, and automatically sync it with MuleSoft VCS.

> [!NOTE] Studio uses the EGit plugin to edit API specifications offline. Studio doesnât support the
> EGit plugin for Mule application projects tracked with your own VCS.

To start creating an API specification project in Studio, log in to Anypoint Platform. See
[Adding Anypoint Platform User Accounts](set-credentials-in-studio-to).

1.  In the taskbar of the Anypoint Studio display, select **File** > **New** > **API Specification
    Project**.
2.  Select the tab of type of API specification project to create:
    - Create a new API specification by selecting the **New API Specification** tab and the type of
      API.

      ![The New API Specification tab and file type drop-down menu highlighted in the New API Specification window](_images/create-api-specification.png)

    - To create a new API fragment, select the **New API Fragment** tab, and then select the type of
      API fragment.

      ![The New API Fragment tab and the type of API fragment highlighted in the New API Specification window](_images/create-api-fragment.png)

3.  Type a name for your project.
4.  Select **Finish**.

Studio prompts you to switch to the API design perspective.

In the API design perspective, use the API editor to write your API specification or API fragment.
Additionally, you can use the API Console view to mock a live service so you can test your API
specification.

Studio uses VCS to track the development of your API specification project and keep it synchronized
with MuleSoft VCS.

## Create Branches

Using a different branch enables you to use a modified API definition in certain cases, without
modifying the main branch in MuleSoft VCS.

To create a different branch in Studio:

1.  In the Package Explorer view, right-click your API specification project and select **Team** >
    **Switch To** > **New Branch**.

    ![New branch highlighted in the context menu](_images/switch-to-new-branch.png)

2.  In the **Create Branch** window, configure your new branch:

    ![*Branch name* field, *Configure upstream for push and pull* option, and *Check out new branch* option highlighted in the *Create Branch* window](_images/create-new-branch.png)

<table><tbody><tr><td><i data-value="1"></i><b>1</b></td><td>In the <strong>branch name</strong> field, type the name of your new branch.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="2"></i><b>2</b></td><td>You can select the <strong>Configure upstream for push and pull</strong> option if you want to also pull changes from MuleSoft VCS into your local branch.<div><p>When configuring this option, you must select the behavior you want Git to have when it pulls the changes from MuleSoft VCS.</p></div></td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="3"></i><b>3</b></td><td>Select the <strong>Check out new branch</strong> for Git to switch to this new branch as soon as you create it.</td></tr></tbody></table>

3.  Select **Finish**.

With a newly created branch, you can start committing your changes.

## Commit Changes to Your API Specification Project

The VCS in Studio tracks every change you make to your project and shows them in the **Unstaged
Changes** area of your **Git Staging** view.

You can track your changes to a file by right-clicking it in the **Unstaged Changes** section and
selecting **Compare with index**:

![*Compare with index* menu option highlighted in the *Unstaged Changes* window.](_images/compare-with-index.png)

After applying your changes, add them to your index:

![*Add selected files to the index* and *Use the Add all files including not selected ones to the index* icons highlighted in the comparison window.](_images/compare-to-index-view.png)

<table><tbody><tr><td><i data-value="1"></i><b>1</b></td><td>Use the <strong>Add selected files to the index</strong> icon to stage for commit the files that you selected.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="2"></i><b>2</b></td><td>Use the <strong>Add all files including not selected ones to the index</strong> icon to stage for commit all unstaged changes.</td></tr></tbody></table>

When you staged the changes that you want to commit, write a commit message in the **Commit
Message** section:

![*Commit Message* section highlighted.](_images/add-commit-message.png)

<table><tbody><tr><td><i data-value="1"></i><b>1</b></td><td>Select <strong>Commit and Push</strong> to also push the change to MuleSoft VCS.<div><p>See <a href="#push-branch">Push Your Branch to MuleSoft VCS</a> below for more information.</p></div></td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="2"></i><b>2</b></td><td>Select <strong>Commit</strong> to add the commit to your local copy.</td></tr></tbody></table>

See [Git Staging View Reference](git-staging-view-reference) for more information about this view.

## Merge and Push Your API Specification Project

Merge the changes made to your API specification to your main branch and sync it with MuleSoft VCS.

1.  In the Package Explorer view, right-click your API specification project and select **Team** >
    **Merge** > **Push Branch**.

    ![*Merge* option highlighted in the context menu.](_images/merge-menu.png)

2.  Select the local branch to which you want to merge your changes:

    ![Local branch highlighted in the *Merge* window.](_images/merge-view.png)

3.  Select **Merge**.

## Push Your Branch to MuleSoft VCS

You can choose to publish your local branch to MuleSoft VCS after you committed all your changes to
your local branch:

1.  In the Package Explorer view, right-click your API specification project and select **Team** >
    **Switch To** > **Push Branch**.

    ![*Push branch* option highlighted in the Mule project context menu.](_images/push-branch.png)

2.  Verify the changes that you are pushing to MuleSoft VCS.

    ![*Push Branch* window options highlighted.](_images/push-branch-view.png)

<table><tbody><tr><td><i data-value="1"></i><b>1</b></td><td>The <strong>Source</strong> section shows the changes that you are pushing to MuleSoft VCS.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="2"></i><b>2</b></td><td>The <strong>Remote</strong> section shows the coordinates to the remote you are pushing to.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="3"></i><b>3</b></td><td>The <strong>Branch</strong> section shows the local branch that you are pushing.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="4"></i><b>4</b></td><td>You can select the <strong>Configure upstream for push and pull</strong> option if you want to also pull changes from MuleSoft VCS into your local branch.<br>When configuring this option, you must select the behavior you want Git to have when it pulls the changes from MuleSoft VCS.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="5"></i><b>5</b></td><td>Select the <strong>Force overwrite branch in remote if it exists and has diverged</strong> only if you know that your local changes must overwrite the changes in MuleSoft VCS.<br>This option force pushes your changes to the branch in MuleSoft VCS.</td></tr></tbody></table>

3.  Select **Preview** to see a summary of the changes you are pushing.
4.  Select **Push**

In certain scenarios, someone else might have modified the version in MuleSoft VCS while you
modified the same version locally. This triggers conflicts in Git.  
See [Solve Conflicts](solving-conflicts-api-projects) for more information.

## See Also

- [Import an API Specification from Exchange](import-api-specification-exchange)
---

# Create a Metadata Class

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

1.  Click the Add button in your Metadata Editor.

    ![Add button highlighted in the metadata editor window](_images/dw-define-type2.png)

2.  Type in an ID for the new metadata type and click Create Type.
3.  Select a data format using the type dropdown list in your Metadata Editor.  
    If the type of your data is undeclared or not understood, the type default to
    `application/java`, and a warning is logged.

Depending on the selected option, there are different ways to define the data structure. With some
formats, the structure can be inferred from an example, others require a schema. For some schemas, a
built-in editor UI is available.

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Format</th><th>Sample</th><th>Schema</th><th>Editor</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p>JSON</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Yes</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Yes</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>No</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>XML</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Yes</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Yes</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>No</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Java</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Yes</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Yes</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>No</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>CSV</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Yes</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>No</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Yes</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Fixed Width</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Yes</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>No</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Yes</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Copybook</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Yes</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Yes</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>No</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Flat File</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>No</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Yes</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>No</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Simple</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>No</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>No</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Yes</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Excel</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>No</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>No</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Yes</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

## See Also

- [Metadata Editor](metadata-editor-concept)
---

# DataSense

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

DataSense is a feature of Anypoint Studio that uses Mule event metadata to help you design
applications. Rather than forcing you to manually discover this metadata, Anypoint Studio
automatically acquires it so that you can map or use this data in your application.

## Understand How DataSense Uses Mule Event Metadata

You must understand how your components use metadata in a flow to ensure data reliability in your
Mule application. This understanding enables you to detect errors early during project design.
Additionally, it helps you identify when a component needs metadata that isnât exposed to it within
its scope, thus promoting data reusability.

## Use Datasense to Map Data

Consider a flow in which you include an Anypoint Connector preceded or followed by a Transform
Message component. The Mule runtime engine uses the working connection to the resource to retrieve
and supply DataWeave metadata about the payload and properties. This feature saves you the effort of
manually inspecting Mule event structure.

For example, imagine that you must connect your organizationâs Salesforce account to Slack to notify
your team about a new opportunity. To do this, you can drop both Salesforce and Slack connectors on
your Studio canvas and configure them to connect to your organizationâs accounts. Then, you can drop
a Transform Message component between the connectors. Mule automatically captures the data type and
structure information from each provider and prescribes the input and output for your data mapping.
With this prescription in place, you can configure the mapping, populating the DataWeave code.

![A diagram depicting a workflow for transforming an object into JSON](_images/datasense-perceptive-flow-design-concept-cf42d.png)

## Use Datasense for Autocomplete

Imagine that youâre obtaining status update data from the Slack connector. You want to log the
message youâre sending to Slack, but you arenât familiar with the property names used by this
connector. Instead of referring to Slack documentation, you can insert a Logger message processor
after the Slack connector and begin to type in it, which activates autocomplete. For example, if you
type `#[payload.` and press `Ctrl+Spacebar`, you get a list of all the properties and methods
associated with that payload. In the list, you also get the properties returned by your request to
the Slack connector.

![The DataSense autocomplete feature highlighted in the payload](_images/payload-autocomplete.png)

## When to Manually Define Metadata

You can manually define the metadata that all the components of a Mule application expose. You must
do this if required (for example, for a metadata type that you defined). Or you must define metadata
manually if itâs most cohesive to an annotated value (for example, when a component uses or expects
a specific metadata type).

When working with flows and subflows, you must declare their metadata contract to specify the shape
of the event you require and the shape of the event you return to the caller. On the contrary, do
not define metadata when using the Transform Message component because DataWeave defines the
contract between the producer and the consumer of data. In this situation, if you explicitly declare
a metadata type, you mask the actual metadata type of the data coming from your Transform Message
component.

## See Also

- [DataSense Explorer](datasense-explorer)
---

# DataSense Explorer

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

The DataSense Explorer is a feature in Studio that allows you to preview the Message data structure
at different points of the flow, while still in design time.

Select a specific element in the flow, then click the DataSense Explorer icon and explore the
structure of the data that enters and leaves it.

![The DataSense Explorer icon highlighted in the Studio menu](_images/datasense-logo.png)

The DataSense Explorer allows you to navigate the Mule Event and the Mule Message. It takes the
known output fields of flow elements and the DataSense metadata of compatible connectors into
account to predict the Mule Event contents at any given point in the flow. You can easily find what
information is available where, even the variables that are implicitly created by certain flow
elements which you might not be aware of.

![An input box with a text field, dropdown menu, and a list of variables](_images/datasense-explorer2-1.png)

## See Also

- [DataSense](datasense-concept)
---

# Deploying a Mule Application to CloudHub

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## Before You Begin

To successfully deploy your Mule application to CloudHub, consider the following requirements:

- The host and port number of your HTTP Listener flow sources are properly configured.  
  If youâre using the HTTP Listener as sources for your flow, you must set its host to **0.0.0.0**
  and its port to **${http.port}**.  
  CloudHub then dynamically allocates a port at deployment time.
- All your external classes and resources are properly declared in the `mule-artifact.json` file of
  your Mule application.  
  Due to Mule 4.x classloading isolation mechanism, all external classes and resources must be
  explicitly declared in the "exportedPackages" and "exportedResources" fields on the
  mule-artifact.json file before packaging and deploying the application.

  See the [How to Export Resources](../mule-runtime/latest/how-to-export-resources) task to learn
  more about working with external resources in your Mule application.

- The Java version selected in your project must match the Java version of CloudHub to avoid
  deployment fails.

## Deploy to CloudHub

1.  Right click the project folder on your Package Explorer View.
2.  Select Anypoint Platform and then Deploy to CloudHub.  
    If you donât have your Anypoint Platform credentials configured in Anypoint Studio, you must
    provide your log in information before continuing.
3.  Configure the CloudHub instance to which your application is being deployed.  
    Anypoint Studio uses your package name as the name for your application on CloudHub. This name
    is part of the domain of your deployed application.  
    For example, naming your application "deployment-1" makes your application public domain
    "deployment-1.cloudhub.io."  
    You can also configure the runtime version. By default, Studio uses the application version
    youâre using in your project during development.  
    To reduce compatibility risks, you must deploy to the runtime version used to create your Mule
    project or the closest alternative.

    Additionally you can configure the amount of worker and worker size for the CloudHub instance
    youâre going to use, enable Insight, static IPs, configure log levels, and properties
    placeholders for your application.

## Redeploy to CloudHub

After you deploy your application from Anypoint Studio for the first time, you can keep modifying
the application code and repeat the steps 1 and 2 to redeploy your updated application.  
When you get to the CloudHub instance configuration window, the "Overwrite Existing Application"
option would already be selected for you. This allows you to redeploy the application.

> [!NOTE] Learn more about
> [alternative ways to deploy your Mule application to CloudHub](../cloudhub/deploying-to-cloudhub).

## See Also

- [Configure Anypoint Platform Credentials](set-credentials-in-studio-to)
---

# Design Mule Domains

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## Create Mule Domains

1.  Go to File > New > Mule Domain Project.  
    You can also right click in your Package Explorer view and select New > Mule Domain Project.
2.  Set a name for your project.  
    This is also the artifactId of your domainâs POM file.

When editing your Mule domain, all related applications in your classpath get updated accordingly.

## Associate a Mule Application to a Mule Domain

1.  Right click your Mule application project in your Packager Explorer view.
2.  Select Mule and Open Mule Project Properties.
3.  Select your domain project from the Domain drop-down menu.

    > [!TIP] The drop-down menu only displays Mule domain projects in your workspace.  
    > To point to an external domain in a remote M2 repository click Customâ¦â and configure your
    > domain coordinates.

Keep in mind that the Mule domains must be either available in your Studio Workspace or installed in
the repository declared on your application project.

## Install your Mule Domain in an M2 Repository

1.  In a terminal, cd into your domain projectâs folder.
2.  Type `mvn clean install`.

## See Also

- [Add Modules to Your Project](add-modules-in-studio-to)
---

# Domain Support

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Anypoint Studio allows you to create, edit and maintain Mule domains to share global configurations
between different Mule applications implementing the same domain.

The Mule Domain configuration editor shows two different tabs:

- Global Elements  
  Here you can define the global configurations that your domain makes available for other
  applications to reuse.
- Configuration XML  
  This tab allows you to edit your Mule Domain configuration XML code using autocomplete.

All modules inside a Mule Domain are automatically inherited by all Mule applications under it.  
Module versioning between your Mule domain and you applications must be consistent. It is not
possible to add a module to your Mule Application if such module already exists in your Mule domain.

Mule domains must be either available in your Studio Workspace or deployed in the Nexus repository
declared in your applicationâs POM file.

When a Mule domain is installed in your Nexus repository, and you configured it as a dependency on
your Mule application, Anypoint Studio can automatically resolve your Mule domainâs modules and add
them to your application project. However, when working with remote Mule Domain projects from
Anypoint Studio keep in mind the following restrictions:

- You canât edit the global elements of a remote Mule domain.
- You canât edit the modules of a remote Mule domain.
- You canât run a Test Connection from a global element defined in your remote Mule domain.

Domains are only editable when they are open projects in your workspace.

## See Also

- [To Design Mule Domains](domain-studio-tasks)
- [To Add Modules to Your Project](add-modules-in-studio-to)
---

# Evaluate DataWeave Expression View Reference

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

The Studio debugger enables you to watch for selected DataWeave expressions in all active
breakpoints. If the expression occurs at any of the active breakpoints, the debugger evaluates the
expression, then displays the result in the **Mule Debugger** view.

![A window with numbered steps that guide you through evaluating a DataWeave expression, clearing an expression, and adding expressions to watch](_images/expression-view-reference.png)

<table><tbody><tr><td><i data-value="1"></i><b>1</b></td><td><strong>Expression field</strong><br>Specify the DataWeave expression to watch for in the expression field.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="2"></i><b>2</b></td><td><strong>Clear</strong><br>Clears all results from the expression evaluations.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="3"></i><b>3</b></td><td><strong>Evaluate</strong><br>Evaluates the expression you specified in the expression field against the metadata of your flow.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="4"></i><b>4</b></td><td><strong>Add to watch</strong><br>Adds the current DataWeave expression as an expression watch</td></tr></tbody></table>

## See Also

- [DataWeave Expression Watches](evaluate-dw-expressions)
---

# Edit Your API Specification Project Exchange File

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Anypoint Studio enables you to edit the `exchange.json` file of your API specification project. The
`exchange.json` file contains metadata required by Anypoint Exchange to describe your API asset.

To edit your `exchange.json` file:

1.  Select it from your API specification project in your Project Explorer view:

    ![An arrow points to the flight-api.raml file in a package explorer](_images/exchange-json-package-explorer.png)

2.  Use the editor to update the values of your `exchange.json` file:

    ![A form with fields to edit exchange.json file information, including name, main file, artifact ID, group ID, asset version, and API version](_images/exchange-json-studio-editor.png)

<table><tbody><tr><td><i data-value="1"></i><b>1</b></td><td>You cannot edit the first item, <strong>Name</strong>, because this is the artifact name of your API specification asset.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="2"></i><b>2</b></td><td>Use the second item, <strong>Main file</strong>, to choose the main file of your API specification asset.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="3"></i><b>3</b></td><td>You cannot edit the third item, <strong>Artifact ID</strong>, because this is the artifact ID of your API specification asset.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="4"></i><b>4</b></td><td>You cannot edit the fourth item, <strong>Group ID</strong>, because this is the group ID of your API specification asset.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="5"></i><b>5</b></td><td>Use the fifth item, <strong>Asset Version</strong>, to update your API specification asset version.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="6"></i><b>6</b></td><td>Use the sixth item, <strong>API Version</strong>, to update the API version of your API specification asset.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="7"></i><b>7</b></td><td>Use the green plus icon to add a new dependency to your API specification asset.</td></tr></tbody></table>

Additionally, you can edit the XML of your `exchange.json` file by selecting the editorâs
**exchange.json** tab:
