2.  Use the drop-down menu next to **Server Runtime** to select a new Mule version:

    ![select project runtime version](_images/select-project-runtime-version.png)

3.  Select **Apply and Close**.

## Uninstall a Mule Version

To uninstall a version of Mule:

1.  In the menu bar, select **Anypoint Studio** > **About Anypoint Studio**.
2.  Select **Installation Details**:

    ![studio installation details](_images/studio-installation-details.png)

3.  Select the runtime version you want to uninstall and click **Uninstallâ¦â**.

    ![uninstall runtime version](_images/uninstall-runtime-version.png)

## See Also

- [Java Support](../general/java-support)
---

# Connect the Java Debugger

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Studio disconnects the Java debugger by default. The following steps show you how to connect your
Java debugger if you need to debug Java code in Studio:

1.  Open the preferences view in the manner appropriate to your OS:
    - In MacOS, either navigate to **Anypoint Studio** > **Preferencesâ¦â** or press `cmd + ,`.
    - In Windows and Linux, navigate to **Window** > **Preferencesâ¦â**.

2.  Expand **Anypoint Studio** in the left navigation bar, and select **Debugger**,
3.  Select **Connect Java Debugger**.

    ![A window displaying preferences for the Java debugger](_images/connect-java-debugger.png)

4.  Select **Apply and Close**.

Note that connecting the Java debugger in Studio can cause performance degradation in your
subsequent redeployments.
---

# Sign in to Anypoint Platform from Studio

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Enhance your security in Anypoint Studio by
[configuring two-factor authentication](../access-management/multi-factor-authentication) through
Anypoint Platform.

Secure access effectively by choosing one of the supported methods:

- Google Authenticator
- YubiKey
- Windows Hello

This configuration protects your development environment and ensures that sensitive data remains
secure from unauthorized access, defending against potential security breaches.

## See Also

- [Adding Anypoint Platform User Accounts](set-credentials-in-studio-to)
---

# Maven Preferences Reference

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

In Studio, you can personalize your preferences when working with Maven. Studio reads the Maven
`settings.xml` file, which details the configurations Maven uses during its operation. The
`settings.xml` file enables you to tailor the behavior of the build process for all the projects on
your system. With the `settings.xml` file, you can configure:

- Dependency and plugin repositories
- Proxy servers
- Authentication
- Plugins

See [Maven setting references](https://maven.apache.org/settings.html) for additional information.

To configure Maven setting preferences, open Studio preference settings. In the Preferences panel,
go to **Anypoint Studio** > **Maven**.

See [Maven Reference](https://docs.mulesoft.com/studio/maven-preferences-reference) for additional
information about configuring MuleSoft repositories.

![*Maven Preferences Reference* window accessed from the *Settingsâ¦â* option, from the *Anypoint Studio* menu](_images/maven-preferences-reference.png)

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Preference</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Display runtime version not available warnings</p></td><td><p>When enabled, it warns you when the runtime version configured in your <code>pom.xml</code> file is different from the one configured in your project.</p></td><td><p>Enabled</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Use embedded Maven installation</p></td><td><p>When enabled, it uses the Maven installation embedded in Anypoint Studio.</p></td><td><p>Enabled</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Maven installation home directory</p></td><td><p>The directory of your local Maven installation.<br>You can only edit this preference when you disable <strong>Use embedded Maven installation</strong>.</p></td><td><p>Empty</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Override settings security path</p></td><td><p>When selected, this option overrides Maven default <code>settings_security.xml</code> path.<br>Selecting this option requires you to define your custom path to your <code>settings_security.xml</code> file in the <strong>Path to settings_security.xml</strong> field.</p></td><td><p>Disabled</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Override Maven local repository location</p></td><td><p>When selected, this option overrides Maven default local repository location.<br>Selecting this option requires you to define your custom path to your Maven local repository.</p></td><td><p>Disabled</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>MAVEN_OPTS environment variable</p></td><td><p>MAVEN_OPTS contains parameters to start up the JVM or to pass memory options to Maven before it runs.</p></td><td><p>Empty</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Base command line for builds</p></td><td><p>The Maven command to execute every time you build or run a Mule project in your local machine.</p></td><td><p>mvn clean package -nsu -DskipMunitTests</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Configure Global Settings

Studio manages the servers used in the global settings and you can configure Studio to use an
external Maven installation. By doing so, Studio automatically detects and populates the IDs from
the `settings.xml` file stored in `${maven.installation.home}/conf/settings.xml`.

![â*Global Settings* option highlighted in the Studio setting preferences windowâ](_images/maven-global-settings.png)

If there is no file on this path, you can create a new `settings.xml` file in another location:

1.  Select **Override global settings path**.
2.  Click **Browse**.
3.  Select your `settings.xml` file and click **Open**. + The IDs are listed.
4.  Click **Apply and Close**.

## Configure Update Policies

You can configure Maven update policies depending on your needs.

![â*Update Policies* option highlighted in the Studio setting preferences windowâ](_images/maven-update-policies.png)

See the `<updatePolicy>` in the
[**Repositories** section](https://maven.apache.org/settings.html#repositories) of Maven Settings
Reference for additional information.

## Configure User Settings

Studio manages the servers for downloading dependencies or plugins, defines the credentials of such
servers when needed, and automatically detects and populates the IDs from the `settings.xml` file
located in `${user.home}/.m2/settings.xml`.

![â*User Settings* option highlighted in the Studio setting preferences windowâ](_images/maven-user-settings.png)

If there is no file on this path, you can create a new `settings.xml` file in another location:

1.  Select **Override user settings path**.
2.  Click **Browse**.
3.  Select your `settings.xml` file and click **Open**. + The IDs are listed.
4.  Click **Apply and Close**.

### Add a Server

To add a new server to your `settings.xml` file:

1.  In the **User Settings** window, click **Add**.
2.  Enter the server ID, and your username and password.
3.  Click **OK**.

![â*Add server* option highlighted in the Studio setting preferences windowâ](_images/maven-add-new-server.png)

## See Also

- [Add Modules to Your Project](add-modules-in-studio-to)
---

# Metadata Editor

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Several elements in your Mule flow automatically expose metadata about the expected input and
output.  
However, given that some components donât expose this metadata, Anypoint Studio features a metadata
editor that allows you to define your componentâs metadata either manually, or through an example,
or a schema.

This metadata is handled by DataSense and exposed during design time on every component of your
flows, helping you debug your application and know if the expected data structure differs from
whatâs truly being sent. It also provides smart autocomplete options.  
To use the drag and drop UI of the Transform Message Component, you must define its input and output
metadata.

All metadata classes that you defined in the current Mule project are displayed in the Metadata
Editor for you to select one if appropriate:

![A form for selecting a metadata type, with options to add, delete, and filter user-defined types](_images/dw-define-type1.png)

When defining the structure through the editor, youâre implicitly creating a schema for that type.
These schemas are stored in your projectâs `src/main/resources` folder, and you can open these and
edit them freely on Anypoint Studio.

After you create a new type, you can associate any new metadata you create with this new type to
avoid having to define it again.

## See Also

- [To Access the Metadata Editor](access-metadata-editor-task)
- [To Create Metadata Class](create-metadata-class-task)
---

# Metadata Propagation Between Flows

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Anypoint Studio 7.12 infers the metadata of your flow so that, when you use a Flow Reference
component to reference another flow, you can set the metadata from your flow as custom metadata for
your referenced flow.

Using the Flow Reference component in Anypoint Studio enables you to automatically rather than
manually propagate metadata in your flow to the flow referenced by it.

When you use Studio to scaffold your flows based on a RAML specification, Studio infers the metadata
of your flow based on your RAML specificationâs types. However, because Union types are not
supported, Studio does not infer your Union type values as metadata.

For example, consider a flow named `flow-1` that uses a Set Payload component to create a JSON
payload:

Actual Metadata Sample

```JSON
{
  "value": {
    "ID": 1,
    "departureDate": "2016/12/20",
    "emptySeats": 200,
    "plane": {
      "type": "Boeing 747",
      "totalSeats": 400
    }
  }
}
```

When you add a Flow Reference component to a second flow, `flow-2`, Studio detects the metadata in
`flow-1` as the actual metadata that the referenced flow receives and automatically assigns it as
custom metadata in `flow-2` if the flow does not already have custom metadata declared for it.

Because the metadata in `flow-2` is set once, it canât update automatically if you modify the
metadata in `flow-1`. For this example, if you choose to modify the actual metadata sample for the
Set Payload component in `flow-1`, you must update the custom metadata in `flow-2`.

Consider now that `flow-2` has custom metadata configured already using the following JSON example:

Expected Metadata Sample

```JSON
{
  "value": {
    "ID": 1,
    "code": "GQ574",
    "price": 399,
    "departureDate": "2016/12/20",
    "origin": "ORD",
    "destination": "SFO",
    "emptySeats": 200,
    "plane": {
      "type": "Boeing 747",
      "totalSeats": 400
    }
  }
}
```

Studio considers the custom metadata of `flow-2` as the expected metadata for the referenced flow,
and the metadata set by the Set Payload component in `flow-1` as its actual metadata. You can choose
to either overwrite the expected metadata with the actual metadata, or customize it so that you
choose what metadata you want to overwrite, and which metadata you want to preserve.
---

# Move Transformation to Separate Files

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

By default, the DataWeave code from a Transform Message component is expressed inline within your
Mule XML file. You can instead keep each in a separate file ( `.dwl` ) and have your XML reference
them. These files are packaged with your Mule application when built.

To export the DataWeave code to a `.dwl` file:

1.  Click the **Edit Current Target** button.

    ![Code showing a list of payment data fields, including coin, payments, and type](_images/dw-multiple-outputs-edit.png)

2.  Select the 'File' radio button.

    ![A dialog box with options for selecting a target file](_images/dataweave-externalfile2.png)

3.  Type a name for your `.dwl` file.
4.  Click OK.

A file is created under the `src/main/resources` folder in your project containing your DataWeave
code.

## See Also

- [DataWeave language](../mule-runtime/latest/dataweave)
- [Transform Message Component](transform-message-component-concept-studio)
---

# Mule Debugger View Reference

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

This section contains a full description of the Mule Debugger View and all its available tools.

![A debugger with a toolbar that has ten buttons and a section labeled ](_images/debugger-reference.png)

<table><tbody><tr><td><i data-value="1"></i><b>1</b></td><td><strong>Terminate</strong><br>Stop the running application.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="2"></i><b>2</b></td><td><strong>Evaluate DataWeave wxpression</strong><br>Click to evaluate a Dataweave expression.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="3"></i><b>3</b></td><td><strong>Next processor</strong><br>Runs the application and stops at the next processor in the flow, even if that processor has no breakpoint.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="4"></i><b>4</b></td><td><strong>Run to processor</strong><br>Runs the application and stops at the selected processor, even if there is no breakpoint at that processor. Emulates the behavior of the <code>run to cursor</code> option in Java debuggers.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="5"></i><b>5</b></td><td><strong>Resume</strong><br>Runs the application until the next breakpoint. If there are no other breakpoints in the execution, it runs until executing the entire flow.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="6"></i><b>6</b></td><td><strong>Start scheduler</strong><br>When configuring a scheduler processor in your Mule application, this option triggers the scheduler according to the frequency set in the schedulerâs configuration.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="7"></i><b>7</b></td><td><strong>Stop scheduler</strong><br>When configuring a scheduler processor in your Mule application, this option stops the initiated scheduler. This function only works if the Scheduler has been initiated using the <code>start scheduler</code> function.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="8"></i><b>8</b></td><td><strong>Add expression to watches</strong><br>Adds the current DataWeave expression to the watch list.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="9"></i><b>9</b></td><td><strong>Remove expression to watches</strong><br>Removes the selected DataWeave expression from the watch list.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="10"></i><b>10</b></td><td><strong>Remove all expressions from watches</strong><br>Removes all DataWeave expression from your watch list.</td></tr></tbody></table>
---

# Configure Private Cloud Edition in Studio (macOS)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Anypoint Studio 7.4 through 7.7 support native integration with Anypoint Platform Private Cloud
Edition (PCE) versions 1.6 and 2.0 and 3.0

Anypoint Studio 7.8 and later only support native integration with PCE version 3.0.0

Starting with Anypoint Studio 7.17, the default JDK version for Studio is Java 17. To ensure proper
functionality, add the certificate to the default JDK version that Studio supports.

- If you use an external JDK, add the certificate to the external JDK that you use with Studio.
- If you use the default JDK Studio provides, add the certificate to the default JDK, regardless of
  the JDK version you use in your project. After installing the certificate, if you have Studio
  open, restart it.

## Configure Your PCE Certificates into Studio

Follow these steps to integrate your Studio installation with the Anypoint Platform components in a
PCE deployment:

1.  Extract your CA certificate using OpenSSL:

    ```bash
    openssl s_client -connect <dsm-name>:443 -showcerts > <certificate_name>.pem
    ```

    - Where `<dsm-name>` is the domain name of your PCE instance.
    - And `<certificate_name>.pem` is the name that you define for the file to which you exported
      your certificate.

      For example:

      ```bash
      openssl s_client -connect your.dsm.name.com:443 -showcerts > my-certificate.pem
      ```

2.  Add your `.pem` file certificate to your Studio embedded Java cacerts keystore:

    ```bash
    keytool -importcert \
    -keystore <cacerts_path> \ (1)
    -storepass <pass> \ (2)
    -alias <alias> \ (3)
    -file <certificate_name>.pem (4)
    ```

    <table><tbody><tr><td><i data-value="1"></i><b>1</b></td><td><code>cacerts_path</code> is your Anypoint Studio artifact.<br>For example:<div><ul><li><p>Java 11: <code>AnypointStudio.app/Contents/Eclipse/plugins/org.mule.tooling.jdk.macosx.x86_64_1.1.5/Contents/Home/lib/security/cacerts</code>.</p></li><li><p>Java 8: <code>AnypointStudio.app/Contents/Eclipse/plugins/org.mule.tooling.jdk.v8.macosx.x86_64_1.1.5/Contents/Home/jre/lib/security/cacerts</code>.</p></li></ul></div></td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="2"></i><b>2</b></td><td><code>pass</code> is your cacerts keys store password.<br>Java default cacerts password is <code>changeit</code>.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="3"></i><b>3</b></td><td><code>alias</code> is the name your certificate gets when you save it in the keystore. For example, <code>my-certificate</code>.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="4"></i><b>4</b></td><td><code>certificate_name</code> is the path to the <code>.pem</code> file generated and saved in the previous step.</td></tr></tbody></table>

3.  Add your certificate to your macOS keystore and set it as always trusted:

    ```bash
    security add-trusted-cert -r trustRoot -k "${HOME}/Library/Keychains/login.keychain-db" \
    <certificate_name>.pem
    ```

    Optionally, you can use the KeyChainAccess UI:
    1.  On your computer, open your `KeychainAccess.app`.
    2.  In the left navigation bar, select **System**.
    3.  Drop the `.pem` file that you generated in the previous task into the list of certificates.
    4.  Double-click your imported `.pem` file and select **Always Trust** from the **When using
        this certificate** drop-down menu.

        ![*Always Trust* option highlighted in the drop-down menu.](_images/always-trust-certificate.png)

4.  In Studio, open the preferences view.  
    Navigate to **Anypoint Studio** > **Preferencesâ¦â** or press `cmd + ,`.
5.  Expand **Anypoint Studio** in the left navigation bar, and select **Anypoint Platform**.

    ![Anypoint Studio highlighted in the navigation bar.](../reuse/_images/studio-anypoint-platform-settings.png)

6.  Select the **Use a Private Cloud Instance URL**.
7.  Type the URL of your PCE installation in the **Private Cloud Instance URL** field.

    ![*Private Cloud Instance URL* field highlighted in the in the *PCE Settings* section.](_images/pce-properties.png)

8.  Click **Apply and Close**.
9.  Restart Studio.

## Configure your PCE User Login

The first time you configure your userâs login, Studio asks you to confirm your PCE certificate:

1.  Open the preferences view.  
    Either navigate to **Anypoint Studio** > **Preferencesâ¦â** or press `cmd + ,`.
2.  Expand **Anypoint Studio** in the left navigation bar, select **Authentication** > **Add**.

    ![*Authentication* option and *Add* button highlighted in the *Preference* window.](_images/preferences-authentication-add.png)

3.  Select **Yes** in the **Invalid Certificate** message that appears.

    ![*Yes* button highlighted in the *Invalid Certificate* message window.](_images/invalid-certificate-pce.png)

4.  Add your PCE credentials and sign-in.
5.  Select **Apply and Close**.
6.  Restart Studio.
---

# Configure Private Cloud Edition in Studio (Windows)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Anypoint Studio 7.12 and later supports native integration with PCE versions 3.0.0, 3.0.1, and
3.0.2.

Starting with Anypoint Studio 7.17, the default JDK version for Studio is Java 17. To ensure proper
functionality, add the certificate to the default JDK version that Studio supports.

- If you use an external JDK, add the certificate to the external JDK that you use with Studio.
- If you use the default JDK Studio provides, add the certificate to the default JDK, regardless of
  the JDK version you use in your project. After installing the certificate, if you have Studio
  open, restart it.

## Configure Your PCE Certificates Into Studio

Follow these steps to integrate your Studio installation with the Anypoint Platform components in a
PCE deployment:

1.  Open your browser and navigate to your PCE instance URL.
2.  Your browser displays a **Your connection is not private** error.
3.  Click the **Not secure** section in your URL bar, and select **Certificate**.

    ![*Not secure* section and *Certificate* option highlighted in the browser](_images/get-pce-certificate-windows.png)

4.  In the **Certificate** UI, navigate to the **Details** tab and select **Copy to Fileâ¦â**.

    ![*Details* tab and *Copy to File* button highlighted in the *Certificate* window](_images/copy-certificate-windows.png)

5.  In the **Certificate Export Wizard** select **Base-64 encoded X.509 (.CER)**, and click
    **Next**.

    ![*Base-64 encoded X.509 (.CER)* option and *Next* button highlighted in the *Certificate Export Wizard* window](_images/base64-certificate-windows.png)

6.  Select a destination folder in your computer and click **Finish**.

    ![Destination folder and *Next* button highlighted in the *Certificate Export Wizard* window](_images/export-certificate-windows.png)

7.  Navigate to your exported `.cer` file, double-click it, and select **Install Certificateâ¦â**.

    ![*Install Certificateâ¦â* button highlighted in the *Certificate* window](_images/install-certificate-studio.png)

8.  In the **Certificate Import Wizard**, select **Place all certificates in the following store**
    and type **Trusted Root Certification Authorities**.

    ![*Place all certificates in the following store* option and *Next* button highlighted in the *Certificate Export Wizard* window](_images/trusted-root-certificate-windows.png)

9.  Select **Next** and then **Finish**.
10. Select **Yes** when the security warning asks for confirmation to install the certificate.

    ![*Yes* button highlighted in the *Security Warning* window](_images/install-certificate-security-warning-windows.png)

11. Add your `.cer` file certificate to your Studio embedded Java cacerts keystore:

    ```none
    bin\keytool -importcert ^
    -keystore [cacerts_path] ^ (1)
    -storepass [pass] ^ (2)
    -alias [alias] ^ (3)
    -file [certificate_name].cer (4)
    ```

    <table><tbody><tr><td><i data-value="1"></i><b>1</b></td><td><code>cacerts_path</code> is your Anypoint Studio artifact.<br>For example:<div><ul><li><p>Java 11: <code>AnypointStudio\plugins\org.mule.tooling.jdk.win32.x86_64_1.1.5\lib\security\cacerts</code>.</p></li><li><p>Java 8: <code>AnypointStudio\plugins\org.mule.tooling.jdk.v8.win32.x86_64_1.1.5\jre\lib\security\cacerts</code>.</p></li></ul></div></td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="2"></i><b>2</b></td><td><code>pass</code> is your cacerts keys store password.<br>Java default cacerts password is <code>changeit</code>.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="3"></i><b>3</b></td><td><code>alias</code> is the name your certificate gets when you save it in the keystore.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="4"></i><b>4</b></td><td><code>certificate_name</code> is the path to the .cer file generated and saved in the previous step.</td></tr></tbody></table>

12. In Studio, open the preferences view.  
    Navigate to **Window** > **Preferencesâ¦â**.
13. Type the URL of your PCE installation in the **Private Cloud Instance URL** field.

    ![*Private Cloud Instance URL* field highlighted in the *Preferences* window](_images/pce-properties.png)

14. Select **Apply and Close**.
15. Restart Studio.

## Configure your PCE User Login

The first time you configure your userâs login, Studio asks you to confirm your PCE certificate:

1.  Open the preferences view.  
    Navigate to **Anypoint Studio** > **Preferencesâ¦â**.
2.  Expand **Anypoint Studio** in the left navigation bar, select **Authentication** > **Add**.

    ![*Authentication* tab and *Add* button highlighted in the *Preferences* window](_images/preferences-authentication-add.png)

3.  Add your PCE credentials and sign-in.
4.  Select **Apply and Close**.
---

# Configure Private Cloud Edition in Studio

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

To configure Anypoint Private Cloud Edition (Anypoint Platform PCE) in Anypoint Studio, follow the
instructions for the version you are installing:

- [Configure Anypoint Platform PCE 4.x in Studio](pce4-configuration)
- [Configure Anypoint Platform PCE 3.x in Studio](pce3-configuration)
---

# Configure Anypoint Platform PCE 3.x in Studio

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Anypoint Studio 7.12 and later supports native integration with PCE version 3.0.0, 3.0.1, and 3.0.2.
Review the following articles for the configuration according to your operative system:

- [MacOS](pce-configuration-macos)
- [Windows](pce-configuration-windows)
---

# Configure Anypoint Platform PCE 4.x in Studio

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Anypoint Studio 7.19 and later adds support for Anypoint Platform Private Cloud Edition (Anypoint
Platform PCE) version 4.0.0.

## Configure Your PCE Certificates into Studio

Integrate your Studio installation with the Anypoint Platform components in an Anypoint Platform PCE
deployment.

### Determine the Java Version and Location

1.  In Studio, go to **Anypoint Studio** > **Settings**.
2.  Select **Java** > **Installed JRES**.
3.  Check the location of the JRE you use: Java 8 or Java 17.

![JRE location in Anypoint Studio](_images/pce4-jre-location.png)

### Export the Certificate Using the Platform DNS

Run the following command by replacing `<ANYPOINT_DNS>` with your actual server URL:

`keytool -printcert -sslserver <ANYPOINT_DNS>:443 -rfc > /tmp/PCE_Certificate.cer`

For example:

```xml
keytool -printcert -sslserver $ANYPOINT_DNS:443 -rfc > /tmp/PCE_Certificate.cer
```

Starting with Anypoint Studio 7.17, the default JDK version for Studio is Java 17. To ensure proper
functionality, add the certificate to the default JDK version that Studio supports.

- If you use an external JDK, add the certificate to the external JDK that you use with Studio.
- If you use the default JDK Studio provides, add the certificate to the default JDK, regardless of
  the JDK version you use in your project. After installing the certificate, if you have Studio
  open, restart it.

### Import the Certificate

Run the following command by replacing `<JAVA_HOME>` with the path to your embedded Java in Studio:

`sudo keytool -import -trustcacerts -keystore <JAVA_HOME>/lib/security/cacerts -storepass changeit -file /tmp/PCE_Certificate.cer`

For example, if youâre using the embedded Java 17 on macOS:

```xml
sudo keytool -import -trustcacerts -keystore /Applications/AnypointStudio.app/Contents/Eclipse/plugins/org.mule.tooling.jdk.macosx.x86_64_1.3.0/Contents/Home/jre/lib/security/cacerts -storepass changeit -file /tmp/PCE_Certificate.cer`
```

For example, if youâre using the embedded Java 17 on Windows:

```xml
keytool -import -trustcacerts -keystore C:[...]\AnypointStudio\plugins\org.mule.tooling.jdk.win32.x86_64_1.3.3\lib\security\cacerts -storepass changeit -file /tmp/PCE_Certificate.cer (1)
```

<table><tbody><tr><td><i data-value="1"></i><b>1</b></td><td>The path changes with each Studio version because the JDK plugin version is included in the Windows path, and Studio includes a different JDK plugin version with each release.</td></tr></tbody></table>

### Set a Private Cloud Instance URL

1.  In Studio, go to **Anypoint Studio** > **Settings**.
2.  Select **Anypoint Studio** > **Anypoint Platform**.

    ![Anypoint Studio highlighted in the navigation bar.](../reuse/_images/studio-anypoint-platform-settings.png)

3.  Select the **Use a Private Cloud Instance URL**.
4.  Type the URL of your PCE installation in **Private Cloud Instance URL**.

    ![*Private Cloud Instance URL* field highlighted in the in the *PCE Settings* section.](_images/pce-properties.png)

5.  Click **Apply and Close**.
6.  Restart Studio.

## Configure your PCE User Login

The first time you configure your userâs login, Studio asks you to confirm your PCE certificate:

1.  Open the preferences view.  
    Either navigate to **Anypoint Studio** > **Preferencesâ¦â** or press `cmd + ,`.
2.  Expand **Anypoint Studio** in the left navigation bar, select **Authentication** > **Add**.

    ![*Authentication* option and *Add* button highlighted in the *Preference* window.](_images/preferences-authentication-add.png)

3.  Select **Yes** in the **Invalid Certificate** message that appears.

    ![*Yes* button highlighted in the *Invalid Certificate* message window.](_images/invalid-certificate-pce.png)

4.  Add your PCE credentials and sign-in.
5.  Select **Apply and Close**.
6.  Restart Studio.
---

# Preview the Output of a Transformation

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

You can provide sample input data to your Transform Message component to see how the transformation
affects it. You donât need to deploy your project to use this feature. A sample output is updated in
real time as you make changes both to the sample input and the transformation.

1.  Click on the root of the input pane and select **Edit Sample Data**.

    ![A dropdown menu with options to set metadata, clear metadata, edit sample data, and configure the reader](_images/dataweave-edit-sample-data.png)

    A new tab opens with an empty scaffolding of your input structure. Values are populated with the
    string `????` as a placeholder.

    > [!NOTE] When the input is of type POJO, samples must be written in DataWeave format. This is
    > not a transformation in itself, just a way to express how data is structured.

2.  Replace placeholder values with sample values to see how they are mapped out to the preview
    pane.

    ![A code editor displaying a JSON file with fields for item amount, payment, product, salesperson, and shop](_images/dw-populate-sample-data.png)

    > [!TIP] To reset the sample to the original scaffolding with placeholders, click the
    > **rescaffold button**
    > ![A menu icon with three horizontal lines and a right arrow](_images/dw-rescaffold-button.png).
    > Note that any sample data you provided is lost.

3.  Enable the preview pane by clicking on the **Preview** button on the top-right of the editor.

    As you make changes in the DataWeave code or your sample data, notice how the output data
    structure changes.

    > [!NOTE] If your transformer has multiple outputs (for example, to both the payload and a
    > variable), the **Preview** pane displays the one corresponding to the currently selected
    > transformation.

4.  Test your transformation for any special characters or structures that might arrive, verify that
    the output is what you expected.

    > [!NOTE] Note that samples defined in this pane work only within the **Transform Message**
    > component, they donât alter the metadata thatâs propagated to other components in the flow,
    > and their values arenât propagated onwards. They arenât used in your running application as
    > default values nor in any other way.

## See Also

- [Workflow: Create a Mapping](workflow-create-mapping-ui-studio)
- [Transform Message Component](transform-message-component-concept-studio)
- [DataWeave Language](../mule-runtime/latest/dataweave)
---

# Propagate Metadata to Other Flows

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Studio enables you to propagate metadata in your source flow to other, target flows referenced
within your application. Depending on metadata already in the target flow, you might need to
configure the specific metadata to propagate from the source flow.

## Propagate Metadata to a Flow Without Custom Metadata

When you add a Flow Reference component to your source flow and reference a target flow with no
custom metadata configured, Studio automatically propagates the metadata in your source flow as
custom metadata in your target flow.

1.  In the taskbar at the top of the Anypoint Studio display, select **File** > **New** > **Mule
    Project**.
2.  Type a name for your new project and select **Finish**.
3.  Drag two flow components to your empty canvas and name them `flow-1` and `flow-2`:

    ![Two diagrams illustrating different flows, each with a source, process, and error handling step](_images/both-flows-metadata.png)

4.  Drag a Set Payload component to `flow-1` and configure the payload:

    ```JSON
    {
      "value": {
        "ID": 1,
        "departureDate": "2016/12/20",
        "emptySeats": 200,
        "plane": {
          "type": "Boeing 747",
          "totalSeats": 400
        }
      }
    }
    ```

    ![A configuration window with a code block displaying JSON data](_images/set-payload-config.png)

5.  Drag a Logger component to `flow-2` and configure it to log `payload`:

    ![A Logger configuration with a payload message](_images/logger-payload-metadata.png)

6.  Drag a Flow Reference component to the end of `flow-1` and configure it to reference
    _\`flow-2\`_:

    ![A dialog box displays an error message and a text field for entering a flow name](_images/flow-reference-metadata.png)

7.  Note that the metadata tab in `flow-2` is now populated with autogenerated metadata, and the
    input tab in the metadata explorer reflects the payload format that the Set Payload component
    determines in `flow-1`:

    ![A screenshot displaying a message flow with metadata and an object featuring ID, departure date, empty seats, and plane fields](_images/logger-metadata-case-1.png)

## Propagate Metadata When Extracting an Operation From Your Flow to a New Flow

When you extract an operation from your source flow to propagate to a new target flow or subflow,
Studio enables you to configure the metadata that you want to propagate to the newly created flow:

1.  In the taskbar at the top of the Anypoint Studio display, select **File** > **New** > **Mule
    Project**.
2.  Type a name for your new project and select **Finish**.
3.  Drag a flow component to your empty canvas and name it `flow-1`.
4.  Drag a Set Payload component to `flow-1` and configure the payload:

    ```JSON
    {
      "value": {
        "ID": 1,
        "departureDate": "2016/12/20",
        "emptySeats": 200,
        "plane": {
          "type": "Boeing 747",
          "totalSeats": 400
        }
      }
    }
    ```

    ![A form with fields for MIME type, metadata, notes, and help, including a text box labeled Value with code inside](_images/set-payload-flow-1.png)

5.  Drag a Logger component to the end of `flow-1` and configure it to log
    `#[payload.value.departureDate]`:

    ![A message with a payload value and departure date](_images/logger-metadata-case-2.png)

6.  Right-click your Logger component and select **Extract Toâ¦â** > **Flow**:

    ![A menu with options for a flow, including extracting the flow to a sub-flow](_images/extract-operation-metadata.png)

7.  Name your target flow `flow-2`.
8.  Select **Metadata Preferences** to configure the input and output metadata.  
    Note that Studio automatically recognizes the metadata of your flow.

    ![A window displays the input and output metadata for a message flow, which includes an object with attributes for ID, departure date, empty seats, and plane](_images/extract-flow-metadata.png)

9.  Select **OK**.
10. Your Logger component is now in `flow-2`, the metadata tab is populated with autogenerated
    metadata, and the input tab in the metadata explorer reflects the payload format that the Set
    Payload component configures in `flow-1`:

    ![A message flow with a metadata section and an input-output view that displays a message payload with object and value attributes](_images/extract-flow-metadata-final.png)

## Propagate Metadata to a Flow with Custom Metadata Already Configured

When you add a Flow Reference component to your source flow and reference another, target flow that
has custom metadata already configured, Studio prompts you to select the metadata that you want to
propagate:

1.  In the taskbar at the top of the Anypoint Studio display, select **File** > **New** > **Mule
    Project**.
2.  Type a name for your new project and select **Finish**.
3.  Drag two flow components to your empty canvas and name them `flow-1` and `flow-2`:

    ![Two diagrams illustrating different flows, each with a source, process, and error handling step](_images/both-flows-metadata.png)

4.  Drag a Set Payload component to `flow-1` and configure the payload:

    ```JSON
    {
      "value": {
        "ID": 1,
        "departureDate": "2016/12/20",
        "emptySeats": 200,
        "plane": {
          "type": "Boeing 747",
          "totalSeats": 400
        }
      }
    }
    ```

    ![A configuration window with a code block displaying JSON data](_images/set-payload-config.png)

5.  Create a JSON file with the name _\`arrival-type-example.json\`_ in your
    `src/main/resources/examples` directory with the following format:

    arrival-type-example.json

    ```JSON
    {
      "value": {
        "ID": 1,
        "arrivalDate": "2016/12/20",
        "plane": {
          "type": "Boeing 747",
          "totalSeats": 400
        }
      }
    }
    ```

6.  Select `flow-2` > **Metadata** tab > **Add Metadata**:

    ![A screenshot displays a message flow with a button to add metadata](_images/add-metadata-flow-2.png)

7.  Select **Input: Payload** > **Edit**:

    ![An arrow points to an icon for editing metadata](_images/configure-input-metadata.png)

8.  Select **Add**, create a new metadata type called _\`arrivalType\`_, and select **Create type**:

    ![A window containing a form for the creation of a new metadata type, including a type ID field and a 'Create Type' button](_images/create-metadata-type.png)

9.  Select the type **JSON** > **Example** and select the **â¦â** button to browse to
    `src/main/resources/examples/arrival-type-example.json`:

    ![A window with options to select a metadata type, including JSON and a text field for an example](_images/create-json-metadata-type.png)

10. Click **Select**.
11. Drag a Logger component to `flow-2` and configure it to log `payload`:

    ![A Logger configuration with a payload message](_images/logger-payload-metadata.png)

12. Drag a Flow Reference component to the end of `flow-1` and configure it to reference
    _\`flow-2\`_:

    ![A dialog box displays an error message and a text field for entering a flow name](_images/flow-reference-metadata.png)

13. Select the Flow Reference component **Metadata** tab, and select **Set Metadata**:

    ![A screenshot of a flow reference panel with the option to add metadata](_images/set-metadata-example.png)

    Studio warns you that setting the metadata from `flow-1` will overwrite the existing
    `arrivalType` metadata type configured for `flow-2`:

    ![A dialog box labeled Custom metadata inquires if you wish to replace the existing flow-2 metadata](_images/overwrite-metadata.png)

14. Select **Customize** to configure the desired metadata to propagate to the flow.

    Note that Studio recognizes the actual metadata as that set in `flow-1`, and the expected
    metadata as that configured in `flow-2`.

    ![A window that displays input and output metadata, with checkboxes for expected object values](_images/custom-metadata-configure-overwrite.png)

    Customizing the actual and expected metadata for a referenced flow is helpful if you want to
    define a specific set of metadata to propagate.
---

# Configure Proxy Settings

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Anypoint Studio allows you to configure proxy configurations for when your computer is behind a
proxy server and needs to connect to the internet to download plugins and install updates.

Anypoint Studio supports configurations for authenticated and non-authenticated proxies.

1.  Open the preferences view in the manner appropriate to your OS:
    - In MacOS, either navigate to **Anypoint Studio** > **Preferencesâ¦â** or press `cmd + ,`.
    - In Windows and Linux, navigate to **Window** > **Preferencesâ¦â**.

2.  Under **General** select **Network Connections**.
3.  Select your Active Provider:
    - **Direct** causes Anypoint Studio to open connections without the use of a proxy server.
    - **Manual** causes Anypoint Studio to open connections using the settings defined in the
      options below.
    - **Native** causes Anypoint Studio to open connections using settings discovered in the OS
      where you installed Anypoint Studio.

4.  Click **Edit** when selecting the proxy entry for each schema to configure its host and port
    number.

    If youâre using an authenticated proxy, select **Requires Authentication** and complete the
    **User** and **Password** fields.

5.  If necessary, you can add bypasses to your proxy. This is a list of hosts that should not use
    any proxy.

    Click **Add Hostâ¦â** to add hostnames to your proxy bypass list.

    Keep in mind that when setting a manual provider, donât disable the default `localhost` and
    `127.0.0.1` bypasses. If you disable the bypasses, the embedded Mule runtime within Studio wonât
    be able to reach your application.

When applying changes to your proxy settings, Anypoint Studio restarts the Mule instance used to
obtain metadata and test outbound connections. If this fails, manually restart your instance:
navigate to **Preferences** > **Tooling**, and click **Restart Instance**.

## Proxy Settings Reference

This section describes the options for configuring network connections from Anypoint Studio.

You can access these configuration options from **Preferencesâ¦â** > **General** > **Network
Connections**.

![Table of Proxy Connections: Connection, Schema, Provider, and Authorization Status](_images/proxy-settings-reference-4eeb1.png)

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Option</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p>Active Provider</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Specifies the settings profile to be used when opening connections.</p></div><div><ul><li><p><strong>Direct</strong> causes Anypoint Studio to open connections without the use of a proxy server.</p></li><li><p><strong>Manual</strong> causes Anypoint Studio to open connections using the settings defined in the options below.</p></li><li><p><strong>Native</strong> causes Anypoint Studio to open connections using settings discovered in the OS where Anypoint Studio is running.</p></li></ul></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><strong>Native</strong> if Anypoint Studio detects a proxy configuration in your OS.<br><strong>Direct</strong> if no proxy configuration is detected.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Proxy Entries</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>This table displays entries that are available for all providers. You can check the boxes in the first column for your selected provider to use that entry.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>none</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Proxy Bypass</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>This table specifies, either by name or pattern, which hosts should not use any proxy. A direct connection will always be used for matching hosts. You can check the boxes in the first column for your selected provider to use that entry.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>localhost</code> and <code>127.0.0.1</code> are marked by default for the Manual provider.<br>Donât remove this configuration when using a proxy. If you do, the embedded Mule Runtime within Studio wonât be able to reach your application.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>
---

# Publish an API Project to Exchange

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

You can publish your API project as a new version of an API specification to Exchange.

To publish your API project as an API in Exchange:

1.  In the Package Explorer view, right-click your API specification project and select **Manage API
    Specification** > **Publish to Exchange**.

    ![A menu that provides options to manage an API specification, including run, debug, and restore](_images/publish-api-project-to-exchange.png)

2.  Configure the following properties for your API to be an Exchange asset:

    ![A form for configuring an API specification to publish to an exchange, with fields for user, specification name, artifact ID, group ID, last published version, asset version, last published API version, and API version](_images/configure-api-spec.png)

<table><tbody><tr><td><i data-value="1"></i><b>1</b></td><td>In <strong>Specification name</strong>, type the name of your API</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="2"></i><b>2</b></td><td>In <strong>Artifact ID</strong>, type the name of your API artifact</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="3"></i><b>3</b></td><td>In <strong>Group ID</strong>, choose the group ID to which your API artifact belongs</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="4"></i><b>4</b></td><td>In <strong>Assert Version</strong>, type the version of your API asset</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="5"></i><b>5</b></td><td>In <strong>API Version</strong>, type the version of your API specification</td></tr></tbody></table>

3.  Select **Finish**.
---

# Secure Storage Overview

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Anypoint Studio 7.10 and earlier versions stored the information of authenticated users in the
workspace, meaning that you could have different authenticated users per workspace.

Studio 7.11 and later introduce Secure Storage, provided by Eclipse to encrypt and save your
credentials.

Studio provides a main password provider that automatically generates a main password for all your
user credentials. When adding a new credential, Studio uses your main password to encrypt and save
that credential into a secure storage file in your file system.

The architecture of Secure Storage enables user authentication to be shared across all Eclipse-based
workspaces and Anypoint Studio instances.

## Main Password Providers

The available main password providers depend on your operative system. Studio assigns a priority to
the available main password providers and enables you to choose which one to use. By default, Studio
assigns a higher level of priority to the password provider that uses your OSâs underlying
capabilities to generate and encrypt your main password.

When adding a new credential to Studio, it automatically uses the main password generated by the
password provider with the highest priority to encrypt your credential, and stores it in a secure
storage file located in your filesystem.

Windows Integration (64 bit)

Available on Windows. This provider creates and encrypts a main password based on your Windows login
information and stores it in secure storage. Windows Integration then uses this main password
automatically to decrypt your credentials.

OS X Keystore Integration

Available on macOS. OS X Keystore Integration uses a random value that is stored in the Keychain
Access app. This provider uses the main password automatically to decrypt your credentials.

Linux Integration (64 bit)

Available in Linux. This provider uses a random value and relies on the `libsecret` library that
must be present and working on your Linux distribution. This provider uses the main password
automatically to decrypt your credentials.

UI Prompt

Available in Windows, macOS, and Linux. This provider prompts you to create a main password and uses
it to encrypt your credentials. This provider requires you to enter your main password every time
you open Studio.

When you add a new credential for the first time, Studio notifies you that a new main password has
been created. Additionally, Studio prompts you to create two password hints to use in case you
forget your main password. The password hints are security questions that you must treat as a second
password. If you lose your main password, your password hints are the only other method to recover
your credentials.

Each provider supplies its own main password. When you use a provider to encrypt a new credential,
you can only use that provider to recover the encrypted credential.

After a main password is created, Studio caches the main password until it is closed, or you
manually clear the password from Studio cache.

## Secure Storage File

This file holds all your user credentials in an encrypted form. By default, the secure storage file
is located in `$HOME/.eclipse/org.eclipse.equinox.security/secure_storage` and is shared across all
Eclipse-based workspaces and Anypoint Studio instances.

## Update the Main Password

You can change the main password for a particular provider. When changing a main password, all the
encrypted data by this provider is re-encrypted with the new main password.

### Before you Begin

To avoid decryption issues, ensure that all other instances of Anypoint Studio and any other
Eclipse-based application are not running.

To regenerate your main password:

1.  Navigate to **Preferences** > **General** > **Security** > **Secure Storage**.
2.  Select **Change Passwordâ¦â**:

    ![A table of secure storage providers, their descriptions, priorities, and instructions to change or reset your password](_images/secure-storage-change-password.png)

3.  When Studio prompts you to create password hints, select **Yes**:

    ![A popup window will ask if you want to provide a password hint](_images/new-password-created.png)

    > [!CAUTION] If you donât supply new password hints, then the existing hints persist,
    > referencing the existing main password, and you will not be able to recover your new main
    > password.

4.  When Studio confirms that the password is successfully updated, select **Apply and Close**.

## Customize the Location of the Secure Storage and Password Files

To change the location of the password and secure storage files:

1.  Locate your `AnypointStudio.ini` file:
    - In Windows:
      1.  Go to your Anypoint Studio installation directory.

    - In macOS:
      1.  Go to your `/Applications` folder, right-click the `AnypointStudio` package, and select
          **Show Package Contents**.
      2.  Go to `Contents` > `Eclipse`, and open the `AnypointStudio.ini` file.

    - In Linux:
      1.  Go to `/usr/lib/anypointstudio/anypointstudio.ini`.

2.  Add the following options to bypass the password provider mechanism:

    ```none
    -eclipse.keyring <file_path> (1)
    -eclipse.password <file_path> (2)
    ```

    <table><tbody><tr><td><i data-value="1"></i><b>1</b></td><td>Configures the location of the Secure Storage file.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="2"></i><b>2</b></td><td>Configures the location of the password file, which Studio uses as the main password.</td></tr></tbody></table>

> [!CAUTION] You can only decrypt your credentials with the same password file used to encrypt them.

## Decryption Errors

### No Password Provided

Exception: `org.eclipse.equinox.security.storage.StorageException: No password provided`

This error occurs when Studio loads and caches the main password for the provider and tries to
decrypt a value that was encrypted with another main password.

This happens when a user of one instance of Studio or another Eclipse-based application changes the
main password at the same time that a user in another instance of Studio saves a new credential
using the previously cached main password.

### Given Final Block Not Properly Padded

`org.eclipse.equinox.security.storage.StorageException: Given final block not properly padded. Such issues can arise if a bad key is used during decryption.`.

This error occurs when the currently cached main password cannot decrypt a value because that value
was encrypted using the previously cached main password.

This happens when a user using one instance of Studio or another Eclipse-based application changes
the main password, and another instance of Studio or the application attempts to read encrypted
values by using the previous cached main password.
---
