# Adding Anypoint Platform User Accounts

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Add your Anypoint Platform user account to enable Anypoint Studio to access resources in your
organization. You can add as many Anypoint Platform user accounts as you need.

To add your account, you must configure your Anypoint Platform user account credentials:

1.  In the top menu bar, navigate to **Anypoint Studio** > **Preferences**.
2.  Select **Anypoint Studio** in the left navigation bar > **Authentication** > **Add**:

    ![The Preferences window displays authentication settings, with an option to Add](_images/preferences-authentication-add.png)

3.  Enter your username and password for your Anypoint Platform user, and select **Sign in**.
4.  Select **Apply and close**.

When adding a new credential for the first time, Studio notifies you that a new main password has
been created. Additionally, Studio prompts you to create two recovery questions to use in case you
forget your main password.  
See [Secure Storage Overview](secure-storage) for more information.

> [!TIP] In every operation related to the platform, you see a toolbar that enables you to change
> accounts and/or business groups, if applicable. You can also add more accounts from this toolbar.
>
> For example, you can add or change user accounts when selecting **Search in Exchange** from the
> Mule Palette view:
>
> ![A form for adding modules to a project, with a username field and an ](_images/studio-credentials-exchange.png)

## Configure Access for External Identity Providers

1.  In the top menu bar, navigate to **Anypoint Studio** > **Preferences**.
2.  Select **Anypoint Studio** in the left navigation bar > **Authentication** > **Add**:

    ![The Preferences window displays authentication settings, with an option to Add](_images/preferences-authentication-add.png)

3.  Select **Configure**:

    ![A login screen for Anypoint Platform with a highlighted Configure button](_images/configure-authentication-idp.png)

4.  Select **External Identity**.
5.  Enter your organizationâs domain name, and select **OK**.  
    Consult your organization administrator if you donât know your organizationâs domain.

    ![A login form for an Anypoint Platform account, with fields for username, password, and organization domain](_images/authentication-external-identity.png)

6.  Select the external identity provider you want to use.
7.  Select **OK**.
8.  Enter your username and password for your external identity provider.
9.  Select **Sign in**.
10. Select **Apply and close**.

## See Also

- [Publish a Project to Exchange](export-to-exchange-task)
- [Import an API Specification from Exchange](import-api-specification-exchange)
---

# Handling Inaccessible Fields

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Improve your Mule project running on Java 17 or later by configuring the Studio debugger to hide
inaccessible fields and methods.

- Hide inaccessible fields:

  Focus on relevant and accessible data to make the debugging process more efficient. Avoid
  accessing fields you canât interact with.

- Hide public methods invoked to retrieve inaccessible field values:

  Reduce the verbosity of the debugger output. When the debugger canât directly access a field, it
  attempts to call public methods that can provide the value of that field. If enabled, the debugger
  doesnât show the method used to retrieve the field value to minimize clutter and noise on the
  screen and make the debugging process cleaner and more focused.

- Use public methods with assignable return types to retrieve inaccessible field values:

  Ensure consistency and predictability in data accessibility and manipulation.

![Inaccessible field preferences highlighted in the debugger tab.](_images/mute-disable-error-breakpoints.png)
---

# Solve Conflicts

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

In certain scenarios, someone else can introduce changes in the version in MuleSoft VCS at the same
time you modify the same version locally. This triggers a conflict in Git.

For example, you might have modified a file in a branch, while someone else modified this very same
file in the same branch directly in MuleSoft VCS. By the time you want to push your changes, Git
rejects the push command because it cannot fast-forward your changes to the version in MuleSoft VCS,
meaning that you must tell git how to apply your changes over a modified version:

1.  In the Package Explorer view, right-click your API specification project and select **Team** >
    **Switch To** > **Push Branchâ¦â**.

    ![A dropdown menu displaying choices for a software development project, featuring Git commands such as pull and push](_images/push-branch.png)

2.  Git rejects the push due to the conflicting file:

    ![A window appears with the push results, including the repository URL and message details](_images/conflict.png)

3.  Pull the changes from MuleSoft VCS into your local version.

    ![A menu displaying choices for a Git repository](_images/pull-changes.png)

4.  Evaluate the conflicts flagged by Git:

    ![A window displaying the outcomes of a fetch and update of flights-api data, with a conflicting result and two merge input choices](_images/flagged-commit.png)

<table><tbody><tr><td><i data-value="1"></i><b>1</b></td><td>Your local commit flagged as conflicting.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="2"></i><b>2</b></td><td>The commit made by another collaborator flagged as conflicting.</td></tr></tbody></table>

5.  Git flags the conflicting parts of the file in the API editor view:

    ![Code displaying a RAML API for American Flights](_images/resolve-marked-conflict.png)

    ```linenums
    <<<<<<< HEAD
              - LAX
              - CLE
    ======= (1)
              - CLE
              - LAX
    >>>>>>> branch 'add-flights-path' of https://anypoint.mulesoft.com/ (2)
    ```

    <table><tbody><tr><td><i data-value="1"></i><b>1</b></td><td>Everything between the characters <code>HEAD</code> and <code>=======</code> are your changes.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="2"></i><b>2</b></td><td>Everything between the characters <code>=======</code> and <code>branch â¦â</code> are the changes in MuleSoft VCS conflicting with yours.</td></tr></tbody></table>

    In this case, another collaborator changed the definition from:

    ```raml
    /flights:
      get:
        queryParameters:
          destination:
            required: false
            enum:
              - SFO
              - LAX
              - CLE
    ```

    To:

    ```raml
    /flights:
      get:
        queryParameters:
          destination:
            required: false
            enum:
              - SFO
              - CLE
              - LAX
    ```

    The conflict happens because git does not know which change to prioritize.

6.  Remove the change you donât want to keep:

    ![A code editor displaying a commit message and a conflict list](_images/push-conflict-changes.png)

<table><tbody><tr><td><i data-value="1"></i><b>1</b></td><td>Ensure that your changes remain in the file.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="2"></i><b>2</b></td><td>Add your changes to the index.</td></tr></tbody></table>

7.  Select **Commit and Push**.

Your copies between Studio and MuleSoft VCS are synchronized with your latest changes.

## See Also

- [Synchronize API Specifications with MuleSoft VCS](sync-api-projects-design-center)
---

# Anypoint Studio FIPS 140-3 Environment Support

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

You can now run Anypoint Studio in environments configured for FIPS 140-3-compliant cryptography.
This ensures your teams keep development workflows aligned with security requirements.

Support for FIPS makes Studio compatible with compliant operating systems and Java cryptographic
configurations.

If your environment enforces FIPS policies, Studio uses approved cryptographic providers and
stricter security controls. Verify that the Mule runtime version you use in Studio is also FIPS
140-3 compliant. Some non-compliant algorithms or legacy settings require updates in project and
runtime configurations.
---

# Anypoint Studio Release Notes

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

These release notes are available for Studio:

- [Anypoint Studio 7.25.0 Release Notes](../release-notes/studio/anypoint-studio-7.25.0-with-4.11-runtime-release-notes)
- [Anypoint Studio 7.20.1 Release Notes](../release-notes/studio/anypoint-studio-7.20.1-with-4.8-runtime-release-notes)

For previous versions of Studio 7.x and 6.x that reached
[end of life](https://www.salesforce.com/en-us/wp-content/uploads/sites/4/documents/legal/Agreements/versioning-back-support-policy.pdf),
see the
[Anypoint Studio Release Notes Archive](https://archive.docs.mulesoft.com/release-notes/studio/studio-archive).
---

# Anypoint Studio Update Sites

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Anypoint Studio uses update sites to update both itself and embedded versions of Mule runtime engine
and MUnit.

> [!NOTE] Corrupted files or incompatible dependencies often cause Studio update errors. To ensure
> stability and avoid residual configuration issues, perform a clean installation of the latest
> Studio version.

## Access an Update Site

1.  Navigate to **Help** > **Install New Software**.
2.  In the **Work with** field, select one of the update sites.

### Add Additional Update Sites

To add a new update site for installing additional components to Studio:

1.  Navigate to **Help** > **Install New Software**.
2.  Click **Addâ¦â**.
3.  In **Name**, add a name to identify the update site.
4.  In **Location**, type the URL of the update site.
5.  Click **OK**.

### Add a Local Update Site

To add a local update site ensure that your update site is a JAR or ZIP file format and complete the
following steps:

1.  Navigate to **Help** > **Install New Software**.
2.  Select **Addâ¦â**.
3.  In **Location**, select **Archive** .
4.  Select the ZIP or JAR file of your update site.
5.  In **Name**, add a name to identify the update site.
6.  Select **OK**
7.  Follow the instructions that appear to complete the update site installation.

## Allow Connection to Update Site Through Firewall

If you are on a network that has firewall restrictions, you must allow the URLs of the update sites
to download extensions:

1.  Navigate to **Help** > **Install New Software**.
2.  Click **Manageâ¦â**. The list of available software is displayed:

    ![A list of available software sites with options to enable, disable, and manage them](_images/available-software.png)

3.  Add the URLs from the **Location** column to your firewall list of allowed URLs.

## See Also

- [Configure Anypoint Platform Credentials](set-credentials-in-studio-to)
- [Add Modules to Your Project](add-modules-in-studio-to)
---

# Configure XULRunner as Studio Default Layout Engine (Linux)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

1.  Download `XULRunner` (we recommend
    [v24.0](http://ftp.mozilla.org/pub/xulrunner/releases/24.0/runtimes/)).
2.  Unzip your package and install it.
3.  Go to `/usr/lib/anypointstudio/` and open your `AnypointStudio.ini` file.
4.  Add a `-Dorg.eclipse.swt.browser.XULRunnerPath=` variable with the full path to your XULRunner
    binaries:

    \-Dorg.eclipse.swt.browser.XULRunnerPath={YourXULRunnerFolder}

    > [!TIP] XULRunner binaries are located in the folder where you extracted the XULRunner package.

## See Also

- [Configure XULRunner as Studio Default Layout Engine (Windows)](studio-xulrunner-wx-task)
- [Configure XULRunner as Studio Default Layout Engine (MacOS)](studio-xulrunner-unx-task)
---

# Configure XULRunner as Studio Default Layout Engine (MacOS)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

1.  Download `XULRunner` (we recommend
    [v24.0](http://ftp.mozilla.org/pub/xulrunner/releases/24.0/runtimes/)).
2.  Unzip your package and install it.
3.  Go to your `/Applications` folder, right click the AnypointStudio.app package and select
    `Show Package Contents`.
4.  Navigate to `Contents > Eclipse` and open your AnypointStudio.ini file.
5.  Add a `-Dorg.eclipse.swt.browser.XULRunnerPath=` variable with the full path to your XULRunner
    binaries:

    \-Dorg.eclipse.swt.browser.XULRunnerPath={YourXULRunnerFolder}

    > [!TIP] XULRunner binaries are located in a subfolder within the directory where you extracted
    > the XULRunner package: `XUL.framework/Versions/Current/`

## See Also

- [Configure XULRunner as Studio Default Layout Engine (Windows)](studio-xulrunner-wx-task)
- [Configure XULRunner as Studio Default Layout Engine (Linux)](studio-xulrunner-lnx-task)
---

# Configure XULRunner as Studio Default Layout Engine (Windows)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

1.  Download `XULRunner` (we recommend
    [v24.0](http://ftp.mozilla.org/pub/xulrunner/releases/24.0/runtimes/)).
2.  Unzip your package and install it.
3.  Go to your Anypoint Studio installation directory and open your `AnypointStudio.ini` file.
4.  Add a `-Dorg.eclipse.swt.browser.XULRunnerPath=` variable with the full path to your XULRunner
    binaries:

    \-Dorg.eclipse.swt.browser.XULRunnerPath={YourXULRunnerFolder}

    > [!TIP] XULRunner binaries are located in the folder where you extracted the XULRunner package.

Additionally, if you are experiencing unexpected crashes when rendering web content, you must
disable **ActiveX Filtering**:

1.  In Internet Explorer, select the **Tools** button.
2.  Select `Internet options`.
3.  Go to the `Security` tab, select the **Internet** zone and click on the `Custom levelâ¦` button.
4.  Navigate to the `ActiveX controls and plug-ins` option and select **Disable**

## See Also

- [Configure XULRunner as Studio Default Layout Engine (Linux)](studio-xulrunner-lnx-task)
- [Configure XULRunner as Studio Default Layout Engine (MacOS)](studio-xulrunner-unx-task)
---

# Synchronize API Specifications with MuleSoft VCS

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Anypoint Studio enables you to import a RAML, OAS 2.0, or OAS 3.0 API specification from MuleSoft
VCS to an API specification project in Studio so that you can edit it offline and then stage and
push your changes as you would do with any version control system (VCS).

This feature only works when importing an API from MuleSoft as shown in the following process.

To import an API specification to write a Mule application implementation see
[Import and Implement an API Specification from MuleSoft VCS](import-api-specification-design-center).

Your API specification project must have a different name than any other project in your workspace.

> [!NOTE] Studio uses the EGit plugin only for the VCS feature of editing API specifications
> offline. Studio does not support the EGit plugin for Mule application projects that you might
> track using your own VCS.

## Import an API Specification From MuleSoft VCS To Edit Locally

1.  Ensure that no other project in your workspace has the same name as the API project you want to
    import.
2.  In the taskbar at the top of the Anypoint Studio display, select **File** > **Import**.
3.  Select **Anypoint Studio** > **API Specification from MuleSoft VCS**.
4.  In the **Import From MuleSoft VCS** window, select your Anypoint Platform username and the
    Anypoint Platform business group where the API specification is located.

    ![A window with fields for importing an API from MuleSoft VCS, including a list of available APIs](_images/username-and-bg-studio.png)

    If you are not logged in, click the **Add Account** button to add your Anypoint Platform
    account. If you have not configured your Anypoint Platform credentials, the username and
    business group options are unavailable.

    If you want to use a different account, click the **Add Account** button and log in again.

5.  From the list of available APIs, select the one you want.
6.  Click **Finish**.

Studio imports the API specification as an API specification project so that you can work on your
API specification project offline.

## Create Branches

You can create your own branch based on the branch you pulled from MuleSoft VCS.  
Using a different branch allows you to modify the API definition without risking pushing those
changes to the main branch in MuleSoft VCS.

To create a different branch in Studio:

1.  In the Package Explorer view, right-click your API specification project and select **Team** >
    **Switch To** > **New Branch**.

    ![New branch highlighted in the context menu](_images/switch-to-new-branch.png)

2.  In the **Create Branch** window, configure your new branch:

    ![*Branch name* field, *Configure upstream for push and pull* option, and *Check out new branch* option highlighted in the *Create Branch* window](_images/create-new-branch.png)

<table><tbody><tr><td><i data-value="1"></i><b>1</b></td><td>In the <strong>branch name</strong> field, type the name of your new branch.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="2"></i><b>2</b></td><td>You can select the <strong>Configure upstream for push and pull</strong> option if you want to also pull changes from MuleSoft VCS into your local branch.<div><p>When configuring this option, you must select the behavior you want Git to have when it pulls the changes from MuleSoft VCS.</p></div></td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="3"></i><b>3</b></td><td>Select the <strong>Check out new branch</strong> for Git to switch to this new branch as soon as you create it.</td></tr></tbody></table>

3.  Select **Finish**.

With a newly created branch, you can start committing your changes.

## Commit Changes to Your API Specification Project

The VCS in Studio tracks every change you make to your project and shows them in the **Unstaged
Changes** area of your **Git Staging** view.

You can track your changes to a file by right-clicking it in the **Unstaged Changes** section and
selecting **Compare with index**:

![*Compare with index* menu option highlighted in the *Unstaged Changes* window.](_images/compare-with-index.png)

After applying your changes, add them to your index:

![*Add selected files to the index* and *Use the Add all files including not selected ones to the index* icons highlighted in the comparison window.](_images/compare-to-index-view.png)

<table><tbody><tr><td><i data-value="1"></i><b>1</b></td><td>Use the <strong>Add selected files to the index</strong> icon to stage for commit the files that you selected.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="2"></i><b>2</b></td><td>Use the <strong>Add all files including not selected ones to the index</strong> icon to stage for commit all unstaged changes.</td></tr></tbody></table>

When you staged the changes that you want to commit, write a commit message in the **Commit
Message** section:

![*Commit Message* section highlighted.](_images/add-commit-message.png)

<table><tbody><tr><td><i data-value="1"></i><b>1</b></td><td>Select <strong>Commit and Push</strong> to also push the change to MuleSoft VCS.<div><p>See <a href="#push-branch">Push Your Branch to MuleSoft VCS</a> below for more information.</p></div></td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="2"></i><b>2</b></td><td>Select <strong>Commit</strong> to add the commit to your local copy.</td></tr></tbody></table>

See [Git Staging View Reference](git-staging-view-reference) for more information about this view.

## Merge Your Changes into the Main Branch

You can merge the changes made in your local branch to the main branch to push to MuleSoft VCS:

1.  In the Package Explorer view, right-click your API specification project and select **Team** >
    **Merge** > **Push Branch**.

    ![*Merge* option highlighted in the context menu.](_images/merge-menu.png)

2.  Select the local branch to which you want to merge your changes:

    ![Local branch highlighted in the *Merge* window.](_images/merge-view.png)

3.  Select **Merge**.

## Push Your Branch to MuleSoft VCS

You can choose to publish your local branch to MuleSoft VCS after you committed all your changes to
your local branch:

1.  In the Package Explorer view, right-click your API specification project and select **Team** >
    **Switch To** > **Push Branch**.

    ![*Push branch* option highlighted in the Mule project context menu.](_images/push-branch.png)

2.  Verify the changes that you are pushing to MuleSoft VCS.

    ![*Push Branch* window options highlighted.](_images/push-branch-view.png)

<table><tbody><tr><td><i data-value="1"></i><b>1</b></td><td>The <strong>Source</strong> section shows the changes that you are pushing to MuleSoft VCS.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="2"></i><b>2</b></td><td>The <strong>Remote</strong> section shows the coordinates to the remote you are pushing to.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="3"></i><b>3</b></td><td>The <strong>Branch</strong> section shows the local branch that you are pushing.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="4"></i><b>4</b></td><td>You can select the <strong>Configure upstream for push and pull</strong> option if you want to also pull changes from MuleSoft VCS into your local branch.<br>When configuring this option, you must select the behavior you want Git to have when it pulls the changes from MuleSoft VCS.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="5"></i><b>5</b></td><td>Select the <strong>Force overwrite branch in remote if it exists and has diverged</strong> only if you know that your local changes must overwrite the changes in MuleSoft VCS.<br>This option force pushes your changes to the branch in MuleSoft VCS.</td></tr></tbody></table>

3.  Select **Preview** to see a summary of the changes you are pushing.
4.  Select **Push**

In certain scenarios, someone else might have modified the version in MuleSoft VCS while you
modified the same version locally. This triggers conflicts in Git.  
See [Solve Conflicts](solving-conflicts-api-projects) for more information.

## See Also

- [OpenAPI Specification 3.0 Support Release Notes](../release-notes/platform/oas3)
---

# Edit Imported API Specifications in Your Projects in Studio

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

You can edit an imported API specification in your project as an API specification project.

Before you begin, ensure that you have the necessary permissions to edit the API specification, and
that you donât already have an API specification project for that API specification in your Studio
workspace.

1.  In the Package Explorer view, right-click the API dependency of your API specification project,
    select the name of the API you want to edit, and select **Import API Spec from MuleSoft VCS**.

    ![*Edit API specification* option highlighted in the dependency context menu.](_images/edit-api-spec.png)

    By default, Studio pulls the `main` branch in MuleSoft VCS. If the API version that you imported
    from Exchange belongs to a different branch in MuleSoft VCS, you must switch to that branch
    before continuing.

2.  Apply the changes to your API specification.
3.  Synchronize your changes with MuleSoft VCS before publishing to Exchange.

    Refer to
    [Commit Changes to Your API Specification Project](sync-api-projects-design-center#commit-to-branch)
    for more information.

4.  In the Package Explorer view, right-click your API specification project and select **Publish to
    Exchange**.
5.  Update the asset and API version:

    ![A window showing an API diagram, project files, and a menu with options including ](_images/update-asset-version.png)

6.  Click **Finish**.
7.  In the Package Explorer view, right-click the Mule project where you imported the API
    specification that edited, and select **API Specs** > the name of your API > **Update Version**.

    !["A window displaying the API Specs section](_images/update-version-api-dc.png)

8.  Select the new version of your API specification and select **Apply and Close**.

Studio updates the API specification to the selected version and asks you if you want to regenerate
the flow based on the new version.

To manually generate a flow for the updated API, right-click the Mule project where you updated the
API specification, and select **API Specs** > the name of your API > **Generate Flows**.
---

# Update the API Spec in Your Project

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

When you import an API spec as a dependency, you can update the API spec version in your Studio
project if thereâs a newer version available.

To update the API spec:

1.  In **Package Explorer**, right-click the Mule project that contains the API spec to update.
2.  Select **API Specs** > the name of your API > **Update Version**.

    After you update the API spec, you can accept or deny that Studio regenerates the flows based on
    the new version.

    To manually generate a flow for the updated API, right-click the Mule project where you updated
    the API spec, and select **API Specs** > the name of your API > **Generate Flows**.

You can manage the API specs in your project that are linked to Exchange from the API spec project
management view by clicking the **Manage Mule Project APIs** icon
(![2%](_images/manage-project-api-icon.png)) in the taskbar.

![The blue dot and the *Update version* button are highlighted in the API property window.](_images/update-api-version.png)

<table><tbody><tr><td><i data-value="1"></i><b>1</b></td><td>The blue dot indicates a new version of the API spec is available.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="2"></i><b>2</b></td><td>Click <strong>Update version</strong> and Studio regenerates the flow based on the new version of the API spec.</td></tr></tbody></table>
---

# Test Your API Specification Using the API Console

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Studio provides an API Console view and a mocking service for the API you are designing. While the
mocking service is enabled, The API Console view returns the HTTP status codes and example payloads
that you defined in your API specification.

Simulating calls to your API is a critical design task that helps you troubleshoot problems and demo
the API to prospective users even before you implement it. You can simulate calls to the API in the
API Console view before publishing a new version of the API specification to Exchange.

Studio mocking service does not modify the `baseUri` field in your API specification when it is
enabled.

## Prerequisites

- You must have an API specification project created.
- Your API specification project must be synchronized with MuleSoft VCS.
- You must turn on the mocking service.

  In your Package Explorer view, right-click your API specification project and select **Manage API
  Specification** > **Turn on Mocking Service**.

  ![A menu that displays options for managing a T-shirt ordering service API, such as enabling mocking services and publishing to an exchange](_images/turn-on-mocking-service.png)

## Steps

1.  Locate the API Console view within the API Design perspective.  
    The API Console view displays:
    - A summary of your API specification
    - All the available endpoints and their allowed HTTP methods
    - A list of all the types defined in your API specification

2.  Navigate through the **Endpoints** section, click the HTTP method that you want to test, and
    click **Try It** to simulate calls to an API.  
    Your expanded API Console view shows the mocking service to the right of the console. **Request
    URL** is populated with your selected API endpoint.
    - The **Request URL** field is populated with your selected API endpoint.
    - Use **Query Parameters** if you need to add specific parameters to your request.

3.  Click **Send** to send your test request.

## See Also

- [Create an API Specification Project in Studio](create-api-specification-studio).
---

# Testing Your Mule Projects with MUnit

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Create, run, and modify, MUnit tests for your Mule projects in Anypoint Studio with MUnit. MUnit is
a MuleSoft unit testing framework for creating and running unit tests on a Mule application, as
described in [MUnit Overview](../munit/latest/).

In the MUnit windows, you can find information about your tests:

- Errors
- Failures
- Results
- Coverage

## Before You Begin

Learn more about MUnit:

- [MUnit Overview](../munit/latest/)
- [MUnit Test Structure Fundamentals](../munit/latest/munit-test-concept)
- [MUnit Event Processors](../munit/latest/event-processors)
- [MUnit Cookbook](../munit/latest/munit-cookbook)

## Get to Know MUnit Tests

You can perform the following tasks with MUnit in Studio:

- Create a test
- Create a test suite
- Run a test
- Debug your project
- Fix errors
- Generate test reports

When you work with your MUnit tests in Studio, you can:

- Find the test of your project in the `src/test/munit` folder, in **Package Explorer**.
- Drag components to your test from the **Mule Palette**, in **Message Flow**.
- Define the MUnit configuration for your tests from **Global Elements**.
- Review test XML files from **Configuration XML**.

## Create MUnit Tests and MUnit Test Suits

To create an MUnit test:

1.  Open your Mule project in Studio.
2.  Right-click on the **src/test/munit** folder, or create this folder if it doesnât exist in your
    Mule project.
3.  Select **New** > **MUnit Test**.
4.  In the **MUnit test suite** window, write a name for your test suite.
5.  Select the flows to test from the list of flows available in your project.
6.  Click **Finish**.

> [!NOTE] You can also configure MUnit tests and test suites from the XML editor.

## Run MUnit Tests

You can run MUnit test in different ways in Studio:

- Right-click on your MUnit test suite file and select **MUnit** > **Run Test**.
- In **Message Flow**, right-click on your test flow, and select **Run MUnit suite**.

When you run a test, Studio creates a build and displays the progress and the result of the test in
the **Console** tab and the **MUnit** window. If all tests execute successfully, the build succeeds.
If there is an error, the build fails.

## Run MUnit Tests in Debug Mode

You can run your MUnit tests in debug mode to step through your code, inspect variables, and
understand the flow of your Mule application.

1.  Open your Mule project in Studio.
2.  In the **src/test/munit** folder, open the MUnit test suite to debug.
3.  In **Message Flow**, add a breakpoint by right-clicking on the element that must have the
    breakpoint.
4.  Right-click on your MUnit flow test and select **Debug MUnit test**.

## Enable Flow Sources

The `Enable Flow Sources` element within the MUnit test tells MUnit which flow sources to load and
execute for the test. By default, MUnit doesnât start the event sources (flow sources such as
triggers and listeners).

You can define flow sources with the **Mule Properties** view of your MUnit test in Studio:

1.  Click **Add**.
2.  Select the name of the flow from **Name of the flow**.

Learn more about flow sources in MUnit docs
([Enable Flow Sources](../munit/latest/enable-flow-sources-concept)) and configure which flow
sources must start for your tests.

## See Also

- [MUnit Cookbook](../munit/latest/munit-cookbook)
- [MUnit Test Structure Fundamentals](../munit/latest/munit-test-concept)
- [MUnit Event Processors](../munit/latest/event-processors)
---

# Download and Install Anypoint Studio (Linux)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

## Before You Begin Installing Anypoint Studio

- To support the Studio Theme on Linux, you must have the GTK version 3 installed.
- Download the Studio installation file from the
  [Download Site](https://www.mulesoft.com/lp/dl/anypoint-mule-studio).

## Installation

1.  Open the extracted Anypoint Studio.
2.  Choose a directory for extraction. You can choose a directory under your home directory, such as
    `~/anypoint-studio`.
3.  Open a terminal window.
4.  Navigate to the download directory.
5.  Use the `tar` command to extract the contents of the archive file. For example, if the file name
    is **anypoint-studio-7.21.0.tar.gz**, run:

    \`tar -xvzf anypoint-studio-7.21.0.tar.gz -C ~/anypoint-studio\`

    This command extracts the contents of the archive to the `~/anypoint-studio` directory.

6.  Go to the extracted directory.
7.  Run Studio.
8.  Click **OK** to accept the default workspace.

## Workspaces Considerations

- You must use an absolute path when defining your workspace because Studio doesnât expand the `~`
  tilde used in Unix and Linux systems.
- If you choose to use an existing workspace, the workspace must be created with Studio 7.0 or
  later.
---

# Download and Install Anypoint Studio (OS X)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

1.  Download the Anypoint Studio installation file from the
    [Download Site](https://www.mulesoft.com/lp/dl/anypoint-mule-studio).
2.  Extract the Anypoint Studio package and move it to your `/Applications` directory.
3.  Open the extracted Anypoint Studio.
    1.  It is possible that Gatekeeper flags Studio as an unsafe application:

        ![You may see an error message that says Apple canât check the file for malicious software and it wonât open](_images/studio-blocked-gatekeeper.png)

    2.  To open Studio, navigate to **System Preferences** > **Security and Privacy** and in the
        **General** tab select **Open Anyway**:

        ![An ](_images/open-anyway-gatekeeper.png)

4.  Click OK to accept the default workspace.
    - You must Use an absolute path when defining your workspace because Anypoint Studio does not
      expand the `~` tilde used in Unix and Linux systems.
      - If you choose to use an existing workspace, the workspace must be created with Studio 7.0 or
        later.
---

# Download and Install Anypoint Studio (Windows)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

1.  Download the Anypoint Studio installation file from the
    [Download Site](https://www.mulesoft.com/lp/dl/anypoint-mule-studio).
2.  Extract the Windows installation file into the `C:\` root folder.

    > [!NOTE] To avoid installation errors:
    >
    > - Ensure that you extract the installation file into the `C:\` folder so that the
    >   `AnypointStudio.exe` file is located in the `C:\AnypointStudio\` folder after installation.
    > - Ensure that your user has read and write permissions for the `C:\` directory.
    > - If you use a Windows antivirus application, ensure that the `plugins/` and `features/`
    >   directories are in the **trusted** category.

3.  Open the extracted Anypoint Studio.

    When Studio first runs, the embedded OpenJDK Platform libraries might cause a Windows Security
    alert.  
    Ensure to allow the OpenJDK Platform library from Temurin publisher through your Windows
    firewall.

4.  Click **OK** to accept the default workspace.
    - If you choose to use an existing workspace, the workspace must be created with Studio 7.0 or
      later.

## Exclude Studio from Windows Defender Scans

While Windows Defender is a tool for keeping your computer safe, it can slow down the performance of
Studio because it scans files in real-time as they are accessed, which causes delays. To prevent
performance issues, you can completely exclude Studio from Windows Defender real-time protection:

1.  Run Studio.

    A Windows Defender real-time protection notice asks whether to exclude Studio folders and
    processes:

    ![âA Windows Defender real-time protection popup asks whether to exclude Studio folders and processes.](_images/windows-defender-exclusions.png)
    - The `/.p2` folder stores Studio plugins.
    - The second folder shows the path of the workspace that you select when launching Studio.
    - The third folder points to the Anypoint Studio application location.

2.  Optionally, select the **Remember my decision** checkbox.
3.  Click **Yes**.
4.  Click **Yes** again when Windows PowerShell requests permission to make the change:

    ![âWindows Powershell popup requests that you confirm the changeâ](_images/windows-powershell-access-request.png)

> [!NOTE] If you select the **Remember my decision** checkbox and click **No**, Windows Defender
> real-time protection scans Studio folders and processes as you work with them.
---

# Download and Install Anypoint Studio

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

You can download two versions of Anypoint Studio 7.x:

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Studio</th><th>Version</th><th>Release Notes</th><th>Mule Runtime Support</th><th>Java Support</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p>7.24.x</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Latest</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><a href="../release-notes/studio/anypoint-studio">Anypoint Studio 7.24.0 Release Notes</a></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>4.10 and later</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>17</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>7.20.x</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Previous</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><a href="../release-notes/studio/anypoint-studio">Anypoint Studio 7.20.0 Release Notes</a></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>4.6</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>8, 11, and 17</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

To download and install Anypoint Studio, follow the instructions based on your operating system:

- [Windows](to-download-and-install-studio-wx)
- [MacOS](to-download-and-install-studio-ox)
- [Linux](to-download-and-install-studio-lx)

> [!NOTE] Anypoint Studio 6.x reached End of Life.

## See Also

- [Salesforce Versioning and Back Support Policy](https://www.salesforce.com/en-us/wp-content/uploads/sites/4/documents/legal/Agreements/versioning-back-support-policy.pdf)
---

# Start the Mule Server In Debug Mode

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

1.  To start the server in debug mode, use the following parameters:

    <table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Parameter</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><div><pre>-M-Dmule.debug.enable=true</pre></div></div></div></td><td><p><strong>Mandatory</strong>. Sets debugging mode in Mule. Issue this parameter first.</p></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><div><pre>-M-Dmule.debug.port=&lt;port number&gt;</pre></div></div></div></td><td><p><strong>Optional</strong>. Sets the listening port for incoming connections from Studio. If unset, the listening port will be 6666.</p></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><div><pre>-M-Dmule.debug.suspend=true</pre></div></div></div></td><td><p><strong>Optional</strong>. Sets "suspend" mode in Mule. In suspend mode, Mule will start, then immediately suspend application execution until it receives a connection on the debug port.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

2.  To pass the parameters to the server, follow one of the two methods outlined below.
    1.  **Method 1**: pass the parameters to Mule on the command line in the appropriate order, as
        shown in the example below.

        ```text
        <$MULE_HOME>/bin/mule -M-Dmule.debug.enable=true -M-Dmule.debug.port=1234
        ```

        The command displayed above starts Mule in the foreground. To exit Mule when it is running
        in the foreground, press `Ctrl+C`.

        To start Mule in debugging mode in the background, issue `start` as the first parameter for
        the `mule` command, as shown below.

        ```text
        <$MULE_HOME>/bin/mule start -M-Dmule.debug.enable=true -M-Dmule.debug.port=1234
        ```

    2.  **Method 2**: include the parameters in the wrapper configuration file,
        `$MULE_HOME/conf/wrapper.conf`, as shown below.

```text
wrapper.java.additional.4=-Dmule.debug.enable=true
wrapper.java.additional.5=-Dmule.debug.port=1234
```

In the code shown above, replace the parameter number to reflect the number of active parameters in
your configuration file.

Before accessing and debugging your application on a remote server, you must first export and deploy
your application to the desired Mule server.

1.  In Studio, click the arrow next to the debug icon (represented by a small insect), then select
    **Debug Configurations**. Studio displays the **Debug Configurations** window.
2.  In the windowâs left pane, click **Remote Mule Application**.
3.  Click the new configuration icon (represented by a blank page with a yellow plus sign) to add a
    new configuration.
4.  Enter the required parameters for the configuration:
    - **Name:** name for this configuration
    - **Host:** indicates the location of the remote Mule server
    - **Port:** indicates the listening port for the remote Mule server

You can debug an application on a remote Mule Server using **Remote Mule Application**. To do so,
you must start the Mule server in debug mode, then connect to the server from Studio.

> [!NOTE]
>
> - When you start Mule in debug mode, _all_ applications that you launch in that server run in
>   debug mode.
> - Remote debugging does not work with clusters.
---

# Transform Message Component

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

The Transform Message component enables you to perform transformations over the input data it
receives. You can explicitly write out a transformation in DataWeave language, or you can use the UI
to build it through dragging and dropping elements.

You can work with two representations of the same transformation, and any changes done to one are
matched in real time by the other.

![A code snippet that demonstrates the mapping of data from one object to another](_images/transform-message-component.png)

<table><tbody><tr><td><i data-value="1"></i><b>1</b></td><td>Graphical View.<br>Two tree views show the expected metadata structures of the input and output, mappings between these are represented through lines. These can be created through drag and drop.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="2"></i><b>2</b></td><td>Source Code View.<br>DataWeave code expresses the transformation in full depth, including functions that aggregate, normalize, group, join, partition, pivot and filter data.</td></tr></tbody></table>

The Transform Message component leverages metadata from connectors, schemas and sample data. It
offers you a preview of your output that is built on sample data and is updated in real time as you
make changes to your transformation.

The Transform Message component has three different editor modes:

- **Show Graphics** mode.  
  The Graphical view displays both input and output data structures and a graphical representation
  of the mappings in your transformation. This mode also displays the Source Code view.

  ![A code block that displays JSON objects for buyer data.](_images/show-graphics-mode.png)

- **Show Source With Trees** mode.  
  The Graphical view displays both input and output data structures without the graphical
  representation of the mappings. This mode also displays the Source Code view.

  ![Here is a code snippet demonstrating how to transform a payload object into a new object with specific buyer data](_images/source-with-trees-mode.png)

- **Source Only** mode.  
  The Graphical view shows only the input data structure along with the Source Code view.

  ![A code block displaying a payload object with buyer details](_images/source-code-mode.png)

You can define your default editor mode in the DataWeave preferences:

1.  Navigate to **Anypoint Studio** > **Preferences**.
2.  Under **Anypoint Studio** > **DataWeave**, select the default editor mode

    ![A menu displaying DataWeave preferences](_images/editor-mode-preference.png)

To improve your experience with the Transform Message component and properly manage your flowâs
metadata, consider the following tips:

- Before creating your mapping, configure everything else on the flow. You can use Datasense to
  improve to expose your transformationâs input and output metadata. See the
  [DataSense](datasense-concept) article for more information.
- To filter what you see from the input or output structures, click the magnifying glass icon
  ![A magnifying glass icon](_images/tips-transform-message-ui-studio-69e11.png) at the top of
  either section and then start typing the name of a field.
- Select an element in the output pane to have its corresponding line in the DataWeave code
  highlighted.
- If an input field is mapped to two or more output fields, you can right-click it and then select
  which of the multiple outputs you want to highlight in the DataWeave code.

## See Also

- [Workflow: Create a Mapping](workflow-create-mapping-ui-studio)
- [Change the Target Output of a Transformation](change-target-output-transformation-studio-task)
- [Add Another Output Target](add-another-output-transform-studio-task)
- [Move Transformations to Separate Files](move-transformations-separate-file-studio-task)
- [DataWeave language](../mule-runtime/latest/dataweave)
---

# Troubleshooting Studio

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

For more details on troubleshooting errors with Studio, you can refer to the following resources:

- [Troubleshoot Issues with Platform Content in Studio](faq-default-browser-config)
- [Troubleshooting Issues with Mule Runtime and Java](compatibility-issues-runtime-java)
---

# Update Your Modules

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Every time Studio starts, it checks for updated modules in your open projects. You can update to the
latest minor version of your module or upgrade directly to the latest version available. Upgrading
to a major version of a module can introduce compatibility issues in your project. Review the
release notes for your modules before upgrading to a major version.

> [!NOTE] If you [select Java 17 for your project](change-jdk-config-in-projects), Studio
> automatically searches Exchange and suggests the modules and connectors to upgrade to make your
> app compatible with Java 17.

To manually check for module updates:

1.  Ensure that you are logged in to Anypoint Platform.
2.  In the menu bar, click **Manage Mule Modules**
    (![Mule icon](_images/manage-mule-modules-icon.png)).

    ![A Mule Palette screenshot with a prompt to open a Mule Configuration file or test to view the palette](_images/manage-mule-modules-location.png)

> [!NOTE] This feature does not support modules that are inherited from a parent POM file.

You can also manage all dependencies in your project:

1.  In the menu bar, click **Manage dependencies**
    ![A stylized letter i in a house shape" 2%](_images/manage-dependencies.png).
2.  Under **Mule Project**, select **Modules**.

    ![Table of modules, their names, versions, and package names, with an arrow pointing to ](_images/manage-modules.png)

    When Studio loads this wizard for the first time, it automatically checks for new versions of
    your modules.

3.  Select the module that you want to update and click **Update version**.

    ![A dialog box will display a list of modules and their respective versions, allowing for selective updates](_images/update-module-version.png)

    Additionally, you can select **Update all dependencies to latest** to update all dependencies to
    the latest version of each.

    ![A dialog box displays the available modules with their version numbers and provides options to update, restore, apply, or close](_images/update-all-modules.png)

4.  Select **Apply and close**.

Studio updates your selected dependencies.

This process works if you manage your dependencies using Studio.

To use Maven properties to specify your module version:

```XML
<properties>
  <http_connector_version>1.5.23</http_connector_version>
</properties>

<dependency>
    <groupId>org.mule.connectors</groupId>
    <artifactId>mule-http-connector</artifactId>
    <version>${http_connector_version}</version>
    <classifier>mule-plugin</classifier>
</dependency>
```

Contact your development team to update your custom Maven code to the latest version available.

## See Also

- [Java Support](../general/java-support)
- [Troubleshooting Issues with Mule Runtime and Java](compatibility-issues-runtime-java)
---

# Update Workspaces

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

When Anypoint Studio runs in a new workspace for the first time, it validates that the components of
the projects in the workspace are updated and compliant with latest Studio 7.12.0 requirements.

To manually validate your workspace navigate to **Help** > **Validate workspace compatibility**.

![Dialog box that provides the possibility to select the updates that you want to perform.](_images/update-workspaces.png)

<table><tbody><tr><td><i data-value="1"></i><b>1</b></td><td>Studio lists all the open projects in your workspace that must update the Mule Maven Plugin version.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="2"></i><b>2</b></td><td>Studio lists all the open projects in your workspace that must add the updated Exchange repository.</td></tr><tr><td><i data-value="3"></i><b>3</b></td><td>Selecting <strong>Perform update</strong> enables Studio to update the repositories in the <code>pom.xml</code> file of the projects you selected.</td></tr></tbody></table>

## Mule Maven Plugin Update for Workspaces Created With Studio 7.1.x and Earlier Versions

Studio simplifies the configuration of the `mule-artifact.json` file. When packaging your Mule
application, this file is used to identify the application classloader configuration and settings,
and the minimum required Mule runtime engine version required to run your application.

Studio includes a new version of the Mule Maven Plugin that can automatically infer some of this
required metadata. This makes it easier for you to keep track of all the declarations you need to
make when exporting resources.

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th><strong>mule-artifact.json</strong> in 7.1.x</th><th><strong>mule-artifact.json</strong> in 7.12</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><div><pre><code data-lang="json">{
  "configs": [
    "ch-usage-sync.xml"
  ],
  "redeploymentEnabled": true,
  "name": "ch-usage-sync",
  "minMuleVersion": "4.0.0",
  "requiredProduct": "MULE_EE",
  "classLoaderModelLoaderDescriptor": {
    "id": "mule",
    "attributes": {
      "exportedResources": []
    }
  },
  "bundleDescriptorLoader": {
    "id": "mule",
    "attributes": {}
  }
}</code></pre></div></div></div></td><td><div><div><div><pre><code data-lang="json">{
  "minMuleVersion": "4.0.0",
}</code></pre></div></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

This simplification reduces the number of declarations you need to monitor during development. A
fully configured `mule-artifact.json` file is still required by Mule runtime engine when executing
the packaged application. You can find the generated `mule-artifact.json` file inside the `target`
directory after packaging.

The plugin inspects the metadata declared in `mule-artifact.json`. If a value is not declared, the
plugin infers it from the application code before packaging. If other metadata is declared, the
plugin uses it to package the application.

If you update to the latest plugin but choose to keep your former descriptor files, you must
manually monitor your descriptor metadata.

When you update a workspace created using Studio 7.1.x and earlier, Studio overwrites your existing
`mule-artifact.json` file. If you have other declarations in `mule-artifact.json` that you want to
preserve (for example, `secureProperties`, or `redeploymentEnabled`), you should back up those files
outside of your workspace, perform the migration, and then restore your old `mule-artifact.json`
files.

Do not to preserve the following declarations:

- `configs`
- `name`
- `requiredProduct`
- `classLoaderModelLoaderDescriptor`
- `bundleDescriptorLoader`

## Exchange Update for Workspaces Created With Studio 7.11.x and Earlier Versions

Because Anypoint Studio 7.12 adds support for comprehensive publication of assets to Exchange, your
projects must add a dependency to the new version of Anypoint Exchange.

When you open a project created with versions earlier than 7.12, Studio adds this new repository to
your project pom.xml file:

```XML
<repository>
  <id>anypoint-exchange-v3</id>
  <name>Anypoint Exchange V3</name>
  <url>https://maven.anypoint.mulesoft.com/api/v3/maven</url>
  <layout>default</layout>
</repository>
```

You must add this new repository to take advantage of the latest publication process to Exchange.

## See Also

- [Mule Application Packaging](../mule-runtime/latest/mmp-concept#mule-application-packaging)
- [Class Loading Isolation](../mule-runtime/latest/about-classloading-isolation)
---

# Use Maven in Studio

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Studio works with Maven to help you streamline the development process, making it easier to manage
dependencies and build applications consistently across different machines.

By default, Anypoint Studio comes with an embedded version of Maven that is used to build Mule
projects. However, you can configure Studio to use your own installation of Maven.

When using your own Maven installation in Anypoint Studio, you can control the version and
configuration of Maven that is used in your project, and integrate your Mule project with other
systems that use Maven, such as continuous integration and deployment tools.

## See Also

- [Configure Studio to Use Your Own Maven](configure-studio-to-use-your-own-maven)
- [Maven Preferences Reference](maven-preferences-reference)
---

# Studio Debugger

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

With Anypoint Studio debugger, you can run your application from Studio in debug mode and configure
breakpoints to stop application execution at a specific message processor. In the **Mule Debugger**
view, inspect the content of the message as it exists at that point in the flow and evaluate a
DataWeave expression against it.

The debugger works with Enterprise Edition runtimes and doesnât connect to Mule Kernel runtimes.

When you run your application in debug mode for the first time, Anypoint Studio prompts you to load
the **Mule debug** perspective. The **Mule debug** perspective is a list of views that help you
debug your application:

- **Mule Debugger**
- **Evaluate DataWeave Expressions**
- **Mule Breakpoints**

## Debug Your Project in Studio

To debug your application in Studio:

1.  Right-click your project in **Package Explorer**.
2.  Select **Debug As** > **Mule Application**.

    Studio requests permission to switch to the **Mule debug** perspective.

3.  Click **Yes**.

Alternatively, run your application in debug mode by right-clicking in the canvas and selecting
**Debug Project**, or by clicking ![A bug icon](_images/to-run-debug-mode-e7b7b.png) (**Debug**) on
the toolbar.

## Configure Studio Debugger

Enable precise control over application debugging by setting the debugger connections and maximum
values. To modify the default configuration values, click **Run** > **Debug configurations** and
select the **Debug** tab.

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Option</th><th>Description</th><th>Default Value</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p>Connection Properties</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Configure the Studio debugger to listen for incoming TCP connections. Click <strong>Suspend</strong> to pause debugger listening activities.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><ul><li><p>TCP connections: <code>localhost</code></p></li><li><p>Port: <code>6666</code></p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Debugger Maximum Consumption Stream Properties</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Control the maximum amount of data the debugger can consume from a stream when inspecting or debugging applications.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>2 MB</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Debugger Maximum Payload Collection Size</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Determine the maximum size of the payload data that the debugger collects and displays during a debugging session. Manage the memory usage of the debugger to ensure it doesnât consume excessive system resources to avoid performance degradation or crashes, especially when dealing with large data payloads.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>100 bytes</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

## See Also

- [Mule Debugger View Reference](mule-debugger-view-reference)
---

# Workflow: Create a Mapping via UI

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

1.  [Define Input and Output Structure of a Transformation](input-output-structure-transformation-studio-task)
    Provide expected input and required output structures.
2.  [Graphically Construct a Mapping](graphically-construct-mapping-studio-task) Drag elements of
    the input and drop them onto elements of the output. You can also include fixed values and
    functions to build out the output.
3.  [Preview the Output of a Transformation](preview-transformation-output-studio-task) Verify that
    the transformation produces the expected output.

## See Also

- [Transform Message Component](transform-message-component-concept-studio)
- [DataWeave Language](../mule-runtime/latest/dataweave)
